<ul><li><span class="nowrap"><span class="noviewer" typeof="mw:File"><span title="Category"><img alt="" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/9/96/Symbol_category_class.svg/20px-Symbol_category_class.svg.png" decoding="async" width="16" height="16" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/9/96/Symbol_category_class.svg/40px-Symbol_category_class.svg.png 2x" data-file-width="180" data-file-height="185" /></span></span>&#160;<a href="/wiki/Category:Renewable_energy" title="Category:Renewable energy">Category</a></span></li>
<li><span class="nowrap"><span class="nowrap"><span class="noviewer" typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:Wind-turbine-icon.svg" class="mw-file-description"><img alt="icon" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Wind-turbine-icon.svg/20px-Wind-turbine-icon.svg.png" decoding="async" width="15" height="15" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Wind-turbine-icon.svg/40px-Wind-turbine-icon.svg.png 2x" data-file-width="128" data-file-height="128" /></a></span> </span><a href="/wiki/Portal:Renewable_energy" title="Portal:Renewable energy">Renewable energy&#32;portal</a></span></li></ul></td></tr><tr><td class="sidebar-navbar"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333133064" /><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1239400231" /><div class="navbar plainlinks hlist navbar-mini"><ul><li class="nv-view"><a href="/wiki/Template:Sustainable_energy" title="Template:Sustainable energy"><abbr title="View this template">v</abbr></a></li><li class="nv-talk"><a href="/wiki/Template_talk:Sustainable_energy" title="Template talk:Sustainable energy"><abbr title="Discuss this template">t</abbr></a></li><li class="nv-edit"><a href="/wiki/Special:EditPage/Template:Sustainable_energy" title="Special:EditPage/Template:Sustainable energy"><abbr title="Edit this template">e</abbr></a></li></ul></div></td></tr></tbody></table>
<p><b>Renewable energy</b> (also called <b>green energy</b>) is <a href="/wiki/Energy" title="Energy">energy</a> made from <a href="/wiki/Renewable_resource" title="Renewable resource">renewable natural resources</a> that are replenished on a <a href="/wiki/Human_lifetime" class="mw-redirect" title="Human lifetime">human timescale</a>. The most widely used renewable energy types are <a href="/wiki/Solar_energy" title="Solar energy">solar energy</a>, <a href="/wiki/Wind_power" title="Wind power">wind power</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Hydropower" title="Hydropower">hydropower</a>. <a href="/wiki/Bioenergy" title="Bioenergy">Bioenergy</a> and <a href="/wiki/Geothermal_power" title="Geothermal power">geothermal power</a> are also significant in some countries. Renewable energy installations can be large or small and are suited for both urban and rural areas. Renewable energy is often deployed together with further <a href="/wiki/Electrification" title="Electrification">electrification</a>. This has several benefits: electricity can <a href="/wiki/Heat_pump" title="Heat pump">move heat</a> and <a href="/wiki/Electric_vehicle" title="Electric vehicle">vehicles</a> efficiently and is clean at the point of consumption.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-1"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>1<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-2"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>2<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Variable_renewable_energy" title="Variable renewable energy">Variable renewable energy</a> sources are those that have a fluctuating nature, such as wind power and solar power. In contrast, <i>controllable renewable energy</i> sources include dammed <a href="/wiki/Hydroelectricity" title="Hydroelectricity">hydroelectricity</a>, <a href="/wiki/Bioenergy" title="Bioenergy">bioenergy</a>, or <a href="/wiki/Geothermal_power" title="Geothermal power">geothermal power</a>.
</p>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Global_Renewable_Energy_Production_by_Countries_and_Sources_in_2023.svg" class="mw-file-description"><img alt="Percentages of various types of sources in the top renewable energy-producing countries across each geographical region in 2023." src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/Global_Renewable_Energy_Production_by_Countries_and_Sources_in_2023.svg/250px-Global_Renewable_Energy_Production_by_Countries_and_Sources_in_2023.svg.png" decoding="async" width="250" height="141" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/Global_Renewable_Energy_Production_by_Countries_and_Sources_in_2023.svg/500px-Global_Renewable_Energy_Production_by_Countries_and_Sources_in_2023.svg.png 2x" data-file-width="512" data-file-height="288" /></a><figcaption>Percentages of various types of sources in the top renewable energy-producing countries across each geographical region in 2023</figcaption></figure>
<p>Renewable energy systems have rapidly become more efficient and cheaper over the past 30 years.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-3" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-3"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>3<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> A large majority of newly installed worldwide electricity capacity is now renewable.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-4" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-4"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>4<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Renewable energy sources, such as solar and wind power, have seen significant cost reductions over the past decade, making them more competitive with traditional fossil fuels.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-5" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-5"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>5<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In some geographic localities, <a href="/wiki/Photovoltaic_system" title="Photovoltaic system">photovoltaic solar</a> or <a href="/wiki/Wind_power" title="Wind power">onshore wind</a> is the cheapest new-build electricity.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-IEA&#95;Renewables&#95;2020&#95;6-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-IEA_Renewables_2020-6"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>6<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> From 2011 to 2021, renewable energy grew from 20% to 28% of the global electricity supply. Power from the sun and wind accounted for most of this increase, growing from a combined 2% to 10%. Use of <a href="/wiki/Fossil_energy" class="mw-redirect" title="Fossil energy">fossil energy</a> shrank from 68% to 62%.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:8&#95;7-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:8-7"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>7<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In 2024, renewables accounted for over 30% of global electricity generation and are projected to reach over 45% by 2030.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-8" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-8"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>8<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-9" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-9"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>9<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Many countries already have renewables contributing more than 20% of their total energy supply, with some generating over half or even all their electricity from renewable sources.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:5&#95;10-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:5-10"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>10<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-11" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-11"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>11<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The main motivation to use renewable energy instead of fossil fuels is to reduce <a href="/wiki/Greenhouse_gas_emissions" title="Greenhouse gas emissions">greenhouse gas emissions</a>, which cause <a href="/wiki/Climate_change" title="Climate change">climate change</a>. In general, renewable energy sources pollute much less than fossil fuels.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:2&#95;12-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:2-12"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>12<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Renewables also cause much less <a href="/wiki/Air_pollution" title="Air pollution">air pollution</a> than fossil fuels, improving public health, and are less <a href="/wiki/Noise_pollution" title="Noise pollution">noisy</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:2&#95;12-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:2-12"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>12<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The <a href="/wiki/International_Energy_Agency" title="International Energy Agency">International Energy Agency</a> estimates that to achieve <a href="/wiki/Net_zero_emissions" class="mw-redirect" title="Net zero emissions">net zero emissions</a> by 2050, 90% of global electricity will need to be generated by renewables.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-13" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-13"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>13<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The current pace of renewable expansion remains far from this required rate globally,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-14" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-14"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>14<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> including in major economies with high financial capacities such as the G7 and the EU.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-15" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-15"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>15<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The deployment of renewable energy still faces obstacles, especially <a href="/wiki/Fossil_fuel_subsidies" title="Fossil fuel subsidies">fossil fuel subsidies</a>,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-16" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-16"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>16<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Lobbying" title="Lobbying">lobbying</a> by incumbent power providers,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-17" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-17"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>17<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and local opposition to the use of land for renewable installations.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-18" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-18"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>18<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-:0&#95;19-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:0-19"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>19<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Like all mining, the extraction of minerals required for many renewable energy technologies also results in <a href="/wiki/Environmental_effects_of_mining" class="mw-redirect" title="Environmental effects of mining">environmental damage</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-20" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-20"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>20<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Some also consider <a href="/wiki/Nuclear_power_proposed_as_renewable_energy" title="Nuclear power proposed as renewable energy">nuclear power a renewable power source</a>, although this is controversial, as nuclear energy requires mining uranium, a nonrenewable resource.
</p>
<style data-mw-deduplicate="TemplateStyles:r886046785">.mw-parser-output .toclimit-2 .toclevel-1 ul,.mw-parser-output .toclimit-3 .toclevel-2 ul,.mw-parser-output .toclimit-4 .toclevel-3 ul,.mw-parser-output .toclimit-5 .toclevel-4 ul,.mw-parser-output .toclimit-6 .toclevel-5 ul,.mw-parser-output .toclimit-7 .toclevel-6 ul{display:none}</style><div class="toclimit-3"><meta property="mw:PageProp/toc" /></div>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Overview">Overview</h2><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=1" title="Edit section: Overview"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">See also: <a href="/wiki/Lists_of_renewable_energy_topics" title="Lists of renewable energy topics">Lists of renewable energy topics</a></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1273380762/mw-parser-output/.tmulti" /><div class="thumb tmulti tright"><div class="thumbinner multiimageinner" style="width:392px;max-width:392px"><div class="trow"><div class="tsingle" style="width:167px;max-width:167px"><div class="thumbimage" style="height:123px;overflow:hidden"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:1990-_Renewable_energy_production,_by_source.svg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/1990-_Renewable_energy_production%2C_by_source.svg/250px-1990-_Renewable_energy_production%2C_by_source.svg.png" decoding="async" width="165" height="124" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/1990-_Renewable_energy_production%2C_by_source.svg/330px-1990-_Renewable_energy_production%2C_by_source.svg.png 2x" data-file-width="1000" data-file-height="750" /></a></span></div><div class="thumbcaption">Renewable energy sources, especially <a href="/wiki/Photovoltaic_system" title="Photovoltaic system">solar photovoltaic</a> and <a href="/wiki/Wind_power" title="Wind power">wind</a>, are generating an increasing share of electricity.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-EmberElectricityProduction&#95;2022&#95;21-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-EmberElectricityProduction_2022-21"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>21<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></div></div><div class="tsingle" style="width:221px;max-width:221px"><div class="thumbimage" style="height:123px;overflow:hidden"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:Global_Energy_Consumption.svg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/Global_Energy_Consumption.svg/250px-Global_Energy_Consumption.svg.png" decoding="async" width="219" height="123" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/Global_Energy_Consumption.svg/500px-Global_Energy_Consumption.svg.png 2x" data-file-width="1200" data-file-height="675" /></a></span></div><div class="thumbcaption">Coal, oil, and natural gas remain the primary global energy sources even as renewables have begun rapidly increasing.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-22" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-22"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>22<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-GlobalCarbonBudget&#95;2025&#95;23-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-GlobalCarbonBudget_2025-23"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>23<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></div></div></div></div></div>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Definition">Definition</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=2" title="Edit section: Definition"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>Renewable energy is usually understood as energy harnessed from continuously occurring natural phenomena. The <a href="/wiki/International_Energy_Agency" title="International Energy Agency">International Energy Agency</a> defines it as "energy derived from natural processes that are replenished at a faster rate than they are consumed". <a href="/wiki/Solar_energy" title="Solar energy">Solar power</a>, <a href="/wiki/Wind_power" title="Wind power">wind power</a>, <a href="/wiki/Hydroelectricity" title="Hydroelectricity">hydroelectricity</a>, <a href="/wiki/Geothermal_energy" title="Geothermal energy">geothermal</a> energy, and <a href="/wiki/Biomass_(energy)" title="Biomass (energy)">biomass</a> are widely agreed to be the main types of renewable energy.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-24" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-24"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>24<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Renewable energy often displaces conventional fuels in four areas: <a href="/wiki/Electricity_generation" title="Electricity generation">electricity generation</a>, <a href="/wiki/Solar_water_heating" title="Solar water heating">hot water</a>/<a href="/wiki/Space_heating" class="mw-redirect" title="Space heating">space heating</a>, <a href="/wiki/Transportation" class="mw-redirect" title="Transportation">transportation</a>, and rural (off-grid) energy services.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEREN21&#95;Renewables&#95;Global&#95;Status&#95;Report2010&#95;25-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEREN21_Renewables_Global_Status_Report2010-25"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>25<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Although almost all forms of renewable energy cause much fewer carbon emissions than fossil fuels, the term is not synonymous with <a href="/wiki/Low-carbon_electricity" title="Low-carbon electricity">low-carbon energy</a>. Some non-renewable sources of energy, such as <a href="/wiki/Nuclear_power" title="Nuclear power">nuclear power</a>,<sup class="noprint Inline-Template" style="white-space:nowrap;">&#91;<i><a href="/wiki/Category:All_self-contradictory_articles" title="Category:All self-contradictory articles"><span title="graphic above shows nuclear as renewable whereas here says not (May 2024)">contradictory</span></a></i>&#93;</sup>generate almost no emissions, while some renewable energy sources can be very carbon-intensive, such as the burning of biomass if it is not offset by planting new plants.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:2&#95;12-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:2-12"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>12<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Renewable energy is also distinct from <a href="/wiki/Sustainable_energy" title="Sustainable energy">sustainable energy</a>, a more abstract concept that seeks to group energy sources based on their overall permanent impact on future generations of humans. For example, biomass is often associated with unsustainable <a href="/wiki/Deforestation" title="Deforestation">deforestation</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-26" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-26"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>26<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Role_in_addressing_climate_change">Role in addressing climate change</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=3" title="Edit section: Role in addressing climate change"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>As part of the global effort to <a href="/wiki/Climate_change_mitigation" title="Climate change mitigation">limit climate change</a>, most countries have committed to <a href="/wiki/Net_zero_emissions" class="mw-redirect" title="Net zero emissions">net zero greenhouse gas emissions</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-27" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-27"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>27<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In practice, this means <a href="/wiki/Fossil_fuel_phase-out" title="Fossil fuel phase-out">phasing out fossil fuels</a> and replacing them with low-emissions energy sources.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:2&#95;12-3" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:2-12"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>12<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> This much needed process, coined as "low-carbon substitutions"<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Suzuki&#95;et&#95;al.&#95;2023&#95;28-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Suzuki_et_al._2023-28"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>28<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> in contrast to other transition processes including energy additions, needs to be accelerated multiple times in order to successfully mitigate climate change.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Suzuki&#95;et&#95;al.&#95;2023&#95;28-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Suzuki_et_al._2023-28"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>28<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> At the <a href="/wiki/2023_United_Nations_Climate_Change_Conference" title="2023 United Nations Climate Change Conference">2023 United Nations Climate Change Conference</a>, around three-quarters of the world's countries set a goal of tripling renewable energy capacity by 2030.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-29" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-29"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>29<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The <a href="/wiki/European_Union" title="European Union">European Union</a> aims to generate 40% of its electricity from renewables by the same year.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-30" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-30"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>30<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Other_benefits">Other benefits</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=4" title="Edit section: Other benefits"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Climate_change_mitigation#Co-benefits" title="Climate change mitigation">Climate change mitigation §&#160;Co-benefits</a></div>
<p>Renewable energy is more evenly distributed around the world than fossil fuels, which are concentrated in a limited number of countries.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-31" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-31"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>31<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> It also brings health benefits by reducing <a href="/wiki/Air_pollution" title="Air pollution">air pollution</a> caused by the burning of fossil fuels. The potential worldwide savings in health care costs have been estimated at trillions of dollars annually.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-32" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-32"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>32<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Intermittency">Intermittency</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=5" title="Edit section: Intermittency"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Variable_renewable_energy" title="Variable renewable energy">Variable renewable energy</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:20240706_Energy_storage_-_renewable_energy_-_battery_-_100_ms.gif" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/20240706_Energy_storage_-_renewable_energy_-_battery_-_100_ms.gif/250px-20240706_Energy_storage_-_renewable_energy_-_battery_-_100_ms.gif" decoding="async" width="250" height="156" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/20240706_Energy_storage_-_renewable_energy_-_battery_-_100_ms.gif/500px-20240706_Energy_storage_-_renewable_energy_-_battery_-_100_ms.gif 2x" data-file-width="1600" data-file-height="1000" /></a><figcaption>Energy from sunlight or other renewable energy is converted to potential energy for storage in devices such as electric batteries. The stored potential energy is later converted to electricity that is added to the power grid, even when the original energy source is not available.</figcaption></figure>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Renewables_need_flexible_backup_not_baseload.png" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/Renewables_need_flexible_backup_not_baseload.png/250px-Renewables_need_flexible_backup_not_baseload.png" decoding="async" width="250" height="192" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/Renewables_need_flexible_backup_not_baseload.png/500px-Renewables_need_flexible_backup_not_baseload.png 2x" data-file-width="4168" data-file-height="3201" /></a><figcaption>Estimated power demand over a week in May 2012 and May 2020, Germany, showing the variability in solar and wind power both day-to-day and month-to-month</figcaption></figure>
<p>The two most important forms of renewable energy, solar and wind, are <i>intermittent energy sources</i>: they are not available constantly, resulting in lower <a href="/wiki/Capacity_factor" title="Capacity factor">capacity factors</a>. In contrast, <a href="/wiki/Fossil_fuel_power_station" title="Fossil fuel power station">fossil fuel power plants</a>, nuclear power plants and hydropower are usually able to produce precisely the amount of energy an <a href="/wiki/Electrical_grid" title="Electrical grid">electricity grid</a> requires at a given time. Solar energy can only be captured during the day, and ideally in cloudless conditions. Wind power generation can vary significantly not only day-to-day, but even month-to-month.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-33" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-33"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>33<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> This poses a challenge when transitioning away from fossil fuels: energy demand will often be higher or lower than what renewables can provide.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Olauson&#95;16175&#95;34-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Olauson_16175-34"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>34<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>In the medium-term, this variability may require keeping some <a href="/wiki/Gas-fired_power_plant" title="Gas-fired power plant">gas-fired power plants</a> or other <a href="/wiki/Dispatchable_generation" title="Dispatchable generation">dispatchable generation</a> on standby<sup id="cite&#95;ref-35" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-35"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>35<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-36" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-36"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>36<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> until there is enough energy storage, <a href="/wiki/Demand_response" title="Demand response">demand response</a>, grid improvement, or <a href="/wiki/Base_load" title="Base load">base load power</a> from non-intermittent sources. In the long-term, <a href="/wiki/Energy_storage" title="Energy storage">energy storage</a> is an important way of dealing with intermittency.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-37" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-37"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>37<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Using diversified renewable energy sources and <a href="/wiki/Smart_grid" title="Smart grid">smart grids</a> can also help flatten supply and demand.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-38" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-38"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>38<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p><a href="/wiki/Sector_coupling" title="Sector coupling">Sector coupling</a> of the power generation sector with other sectors may increase flexibility: for example the transport sector can be coupled by charging electric vehicles and sending electricity from <a href="/wiki/Vehicle-to-grid" title="Vehicle-to-grid">vehicle to grid</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-39" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-39"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>39<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Similarly the industry sector can be coupled by hydrogen produced by electrolysis,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-40" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-40"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>40<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and the buildings sector by thermal energy storage for space heating and cooling.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-41" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-41"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>41<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Building overcapacity for wind and solar generation can help ensure sufficient electricity production even during poor weather. In optimal weather, it may be necessary to curtail energy generation if it is not possible to use or store excess electricity.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-42" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-42"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>42<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Electrical_energy_storage">Electrical energy storage</h4><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=6" title="Edit section: Electrical energy storage"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main articles: <a href="/wiki/Energy_storage" title="Energy storage">Energy storage</a> and <a href="/wiki/Grid_energy_storage" title="Grid energy storage">Grid energy storage</a></div>
<p>Electrical energy storage is a collection of methods used to store electrical energy. Electrical energy is stored during times when production (especially from intermittent sources such as <a href="/wiki/Wind_power" title="Wind power">wind power</a>, <a href="/wiki/Tidal_power" title="Tidal power">tidal power</a>, <a href="/wiki/Solar_power" title="Solar power">solar power</a>) exceeds consumption, and returned to the <a href="/wiki/Power_grid" class="mw-redirect" title="Power grid">grid</a> when production falls below consumption. <a href="/wiki/Pumped-storage_hydroelectricity" title="Pumped-storage hydroelectricity">Pumped-storage hydroelectricity</a> accounts for more than 85% of all <a href="/wiki/Grid_energy_storage" title="Grid energy storage">grid power storage</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-43" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-43"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>43<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Batteries are increasingly being deployed for storage<sup id="cite&#95;ref-44" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-44"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>44<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and grid <a href="/wiki/Ancillary_services" title="Ancillary services">ancillary services</a><sup id="cite&#95;ref-45" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-45"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>45<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and for domestic storage.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-46" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-46"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>46<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Green_hydrogen" title="Green hydrogen">Green hydrogen</a> is a more economical means of long-term renewable energy storage, in terms of <a href="/wiki/Capital_expenditure" title="Capital expenditure">capital expenditures</a> compared to pumped hydroelectric or batteries.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-47" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-47"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>47<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-48" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-48"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>48<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Energy_supply_security">Energy supply security</h4><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=7" title="Edit section: Energy supply security"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>Two main renewable energy sources - solar power and wind power - are usually deployed in <a href="/wiki/Distributed_generation" title="Distributed generation">distributed generation</a> architecture, which offers specific benefits and comes with specific risks.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-49" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-49"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>49<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Notable risks are associated with centralisation of 90% of the supply chains in a single country (China) in the photovoltaic sector.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-50" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-50"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>50<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Mass-scale installation of photovoltaic <a href="/wiki/Power_inverter" title="Power inverter">power inverters</a> with remote control, <a href="/wiki/Vulnerability_(computer_security)" title="Vulnerability (computer security)">security vulnerabilities</a> and <a href="/wiki/Web_shell" title="Web shell">backdoors</a> results in cyberattacks that can disable generation from millions of physically decentralised panels, resulting in disappearance of hundreds of gigawatts of installed power from the grid in one moment.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-51" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-51"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>51<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-52" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-52"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>52<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Similar attacks have targeted wind power farms through vulnerabilities in their remote control and monitoring systems.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-53" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-53"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>53<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The European <a href="/wiki/Cyber-security_regulation" title="Cyber-security regulation">NIS2 directive</a> partially responds to these challenges by extending the scope of cybersecurity regulations to the energy generation market.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-54" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-54"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>54<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Recent analyses indicate that global solar photovoltaic capacity surpassed 1 terawatt in 2024, providing about 6–7% of global electricity supply.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-55" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-55"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>55<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Renewable energy infrastructure is also increasingly vulnerable to extreme weather events linked to climate change, such as heat waves, wildfires, severe storms, and flooding. Solar farms can experience reduced output during prolonged heat or smoke conditions, while wind turbines may require shutdowns during high-wind events or face damage from icing or wave action. These climate-related stresses can threaten the continuity of electricity supply in regions with high shares of variable renewables. As a result, governments and grid operators are adopting climate-resilience standards, hardening infrastructure, and developing emergency-response protocols to maintain energy security under more frequent and severe weather conditions.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-56" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-56"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>56<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Mainstream_technologies">Mainstream technologies</h2><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=8" title="Edit section: Mainstream technologies"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<div class="OWIDSlider" data-owidslider-config="&#91;&#123;&quot;startingView&quot;:&quot;World&quot;,&quot;caption&quot;:&quot;OWIDSlider-caption-0.85048212243732&quot;,&quot;loop&quot;:false,&quot;title&quot;:&quot;&quot;,&quot;list&quot;:&quot;Template:OWID/Share electricity renewables#gallery&quot;,&quot;location&quot;:&quot;commons&quot;,&quot;file&quot;:&quot;&#91;&#91;File:Share electricity renewables, World, 2025 (cropped).svg&#124;link=&#124;thumb&#124;upright=1.6&#124;right&#124;Share of electricity from renewables&#93;&#93;&quot;,&quot;language&quot;:&quot;&quot;,&quot;start&quot;:2025&#125;&#93;"><figure class="mw-default-size mw-halign-right" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><span><img resource="/wiki/File:Share_electricity_renewables,_World,_2025_(cropped).svg" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Share_electricity_renewables%2C_World%2C_2025_%28cropped%29.svg/500px-Share_electricity_renewables%2C_World%2C_2025_%28cropped%29.svg.png" decoding="async" width="400" height="263" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Share_electricity_renewables%2C_World%2C_2025_%28cropped%29.svg/960px-Share_electricity_renewables%2C_World%2C_2025_%28cropped%29.svg.png 2x" data-file-width="850" data-file-height="558" /></span><figcaption>Share of electricity from renewables</figcaption></figure></div><div id="OWIDSlider-caption-0.85048212243732" style="display: none"></div>

<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Solar_energy">Solar energy</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=9" title="Edit section: Solar energy"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main articles: <a href="/wiki/Solar_energy" title="Solar energy">Solar energy</a>, <a href="/wiki/Solar_power" title="Solar power">Solar power</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Outline_of_solar_energy" title="Outline of solar energy">Outline of solar energy</a></div>

<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1273380762/mw-parser-output/.tmulti" /><div class="thumb tmulti tright"><div class="thumbinner multiimageinner" style="width:392px;max-width:392px"><div class="trow"><div class="tsingle" style="width:191px;max-width:191px"><div class="thumbimage" style="height:146px;overflow:hidden"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:SolarFachwerkhaus.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/SolarFachwerkhaus.jpg/250px-SolarFachwerkhaus.jpg" decoding="async" width="189" height="146" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/SolarFachwerkhaus.jpg/500px-SolarFachwerkhaus.jpg 2x" data-file-width="1097" data-file-height="848" /></a></span></div><div class="thumbcaption">A small, rooftop <a href="/wiki/PV_system" class="mw-redirect" title="PV system">PV system</a> in <a href="/wiki/Bonn" title="Bonn">Bonn</a>, Germany</div></div><div class="tsingle" style="width:197px;max-width:197px"><div class="thumbimage" style="height:146px;overflow:hidden"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:Mount_Komekura_Photovoltaic_power_plant_Jan2012.JPG" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Mount_Komekura_Photovoltaic_power_plant_Jan2012.JPG/250px-Mount_Komekura_Photovoltaic_power_plant_Jan2012.JPG" decoding="async" width="195" height="146" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Mount_Komekura_Photovoltaic_power_plant_Jan2012.JPG/500px-Mount_Komekura_Photovoltaic_power_plant_Jan2012.JPG 2x" data-file-width="4000" data-file-height="3000" /></a></span></div><div class="thumbcaption"><a href="/wiki/Komekurayama_Solar_Power_Plant" title="Komekurayama Solar Power Plant">Komekurayama</a> <a href="/wiki/Photovoltaic_power_station" title="Photovoltaic power station">photovoltaic power station</a> in <a href="/wiki/Kofu" class="mw-redirect" title="Kofu">Kofu</a>, Japan</div></div></div></div></div>
<p>Solar power produced around 1.3 terrawatt-hours (TWh) worldwide in 2022,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:5&#95;10-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:5-10"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>10<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> representing 4.6% of the world's electricity. Almost all of this growth has happened since 2010.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-62" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-62"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>62<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Solar energy can be harnessed anywhere that receives sunlight; however, the amount of solar energy that can be harnessed for electricity generation is influenced by <a href="/wiki/Weather" title="Weather">weather conditions</a>, geographic location and time of day.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-63" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-63"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>63<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>There are two mainstream ways of harnessing solar energy: <a href="/wiki/Solar_thermal_energy" title="Solar thermal energy">solar thermal</a>, which converts solar energy into heat; and <a href="/wiki/Photovoltaics" title="Photovoltaics">photovoltaics</a> (PV), which converts it into electricity.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:2&#95;12-4" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:2-12"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>12<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> PV is far more widespread, accounting for around two thirds of the global solar energy capacity as of 2022.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:4&#95;64-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:4-64"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>64<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> It is also growing at a much faster rate, with 170 GW newly installed capacity in 2021,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-65" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-65"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>65<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> compared to 25 GW of solar thermal.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:4&#95;64-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:4-64"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>64<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p><a href="/wiki/Passive_solar" class="mw-redirect" title="Passive solar">Passive solar</a> refers to a range of construction strategies and technologies that aim to optimize the distribution of solar heat in a building. Examples include <a href="/wiki/Solar_chimney" title="Solar chimney">solar chimneys</a>,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:2&#95;12-5" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:2-12"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>12<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> orienting a building to the sun, using <a href="/wiki/Thermal_mass" title="Thermal mass">construction materials that can store heat</a>, and designing spaces that <a href="/wiki/Ventilation_(architecture)" title="Ventilation (architecture)">naturally circulate air</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-66" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-66"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>66<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>From 2020 to 2022, solar technology investments almost doubled from USD 162 billion to USD 308 billion, driven by the sector's increasing maturity and cost reductions, particularly in solar photovoltaic (PV), which accounted for 90% of total investments. China and the United States were the main recipients, collectively making up about half of all solar investments since 2013. Despite reductions in Japan and India due to policy changes and <a href="/wiki/COVID-19" title="COVID-19">COVID-19</a>, growth in China, the United States, and a significant increase from Vietnam's feed-in tariff program offset these declines. Globally, the solar sector added 714 gigawatts (GW) of solar PV and <a href="/wiki/Concentrated_solar_power" title="Concentrated solar power">concentrated solar power</a> (CSP) capacity between 2013 and 2021, with a notable rise in large-scale solar heating installations in 2021, especially in China, Europe, Turkey, and Mexico.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:1&#95;67-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:1-67"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>67<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In 2023, global solar power capacity grew by nearly 30%, driven by falling panel prices and expanded government incentives worldwide.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-68" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-68"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>68<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Photovoltaics">Photovoltaics</h4><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=10" title="Edit section: Photovoltaics"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main articles: <a href="/wiki/Growth_of_photovoltaics" title="Growth of photovoltaics">Growth of photovoltaics</a>, <a href="/wiki/Solar_power_by_country" title="Solar power by country">Solar power by country</a>, and <a href="/wiki/List_of_photovoltaic_power_stations" title="List of photovoltaic power stations">List of photovoltaic power stations</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:1975_%E2%80%93_Price_of_solar_panels_as_a_function_of_cumulative_installed_capacity.svg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/1975_%E2%80%93_Price_of_solar_panels_as_a_function_of_cumulative_installed_capacity.svg/250px-1975_%E2%80%93_Price_of_solar_panels_as_a_function_of_cumulative_installed_capacity.svg.png" decoding="async" width="250" height="188" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/1975_%E2%80%93_Price_of_solar_panels_as_a_function_of_cumulative_installed_capacity.svg/500px-1975_%E2%80%93_Price_of_solar_panels_as_a_function_of_cumulative_installed_capacity.svg.png 2x" data-file-width="1000" data-file-height="750" /></a><figcaption><a href="/wiki/Swanson%27s_law" title="Swanson&#39;s law">Swanson's law</a>–stating that solar module prices have dropped about 20% for each doubling of installed capacity—defines the "<a href="/wiki/Experience_curve_effects" class="mw-redirect" title="Experience curve effects">learning rate</a>" of <a href="/wiki/Photovoltaics" title="Photovoltaics">solar photovoltaics</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-SolarPVlearningCurve&#95;69-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-SolarPVlearningCurve-69"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>69<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-70" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-70"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>70<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></figcaption></figure>
<p>A <a href="/wiki/Photovoltaic_system" title="Photovoltaic system">photovoltaic system</a>, consisting of <a href="/wiki/Solar_cell" title="Solar cell">solar cells</a> assembled into <a href="/wiki/Solar_panel" title="Solar panel">panels</a>, converts light into electrical <a href="/wiki/Direct_current" title="Direct current">direct current</a> via the <a href="/wiki/Photoelectric_effect" title="Photoelectric effect">photoelectric effect</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-71" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-71"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>71<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-72" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-72"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>72<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> 
</p><p>PV systems range from small, residential and commercial <a href="/wiki/Rooftop_solar_power" title="Rooftop solar power">rooftop</a> or <a href="/wiki/Building-integrated_photovoltaics" title="Building-integrated photovoltaics">building integrated</a> installations,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-73" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-73"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>73<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-74" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-74"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>74<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-75" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-75"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>75<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> to large utility-scale <a href="/wiki/Photovoltaic_power_station" title="Photovoltaic power station">photovoltaic power station</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-76" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-76"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>76<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-77" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-77"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>77<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-78" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-78"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>78<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> A household's solar panels can either be used for just that household or, if connected to an electrical grid, can be aggregated with millions of others.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-79" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-79"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>79<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-80" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-80"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>80<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-81" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-81"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>81<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The first utility-scale solar power plant was built in 1982 in <a href="/wiki/Hesperia,_California" title="Hesperia, California">Hesperia, California</a> by <a href="/wiki/ARCO" title="ARCO">ARCO</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-82" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-82"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>82<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-83" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-83"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>83<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The plant was not profitable and was sold eight years later.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-84" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-84"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>84<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> However, over the following decades, PV cells became significantly more efficient and cheaper.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-deutsche-2015-chasm&#95;85-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-deutsche-2015-chasm-85"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>85<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> As a result, PV adoption has grown exponentially since 2010.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-86" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-86"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>86<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Global capacity increased from 230 GW at the end of 2015 to 890 GW in 2021.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-IRENA2018&#95;87-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-IRENA2018-87"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>87<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> PV grew fastest in China between 2016 and 2021, adding 560 GW, more than all advanced economies combined.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-IEA-3&#95;88-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-IEA-3-88"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>88<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Four of the ten biggest solar power stations are in China, including the biggest, <a href="/wiki/Huanghe_Hydropower_Golmud_Solar_Park" title="Huanghe Hydropower Golmud Solar Park">Golmud Solar Park</a> in China.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-89" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-89"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>89<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p><a href="/wiki/Solar_cell#Recycling" title="Solar cell">Solar panels are recycled</a> to reduce <a href="/wiki/Electronic_waste" title="Electronic waste">electronic waste</a> and create a source for materials that would otherwise need to be mined,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-90" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-90"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>90<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> but such business is still small and work is ongoing to improve and scale-up the process.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-techrev&#95;91-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-techrev-91"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>91<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-92" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-92"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>92<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-93" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-93"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>93<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Solar_thermal">Solar thermal</h4><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=11" title="Edit section: Solar thermal"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Solar_thermal_energy" title="Solar thermal energy">Solar thermal energy</a></div>
<p>Unlike photovoltaic cells that convert sunlight directly into electricity, <a href="/wiki/Solar_thermal_system" class="mw-redirect" title="Solar thermal system">solar thermal systems</a> convert it into heat. They use mirrors or lenses to concentrate sunlight onto a receiver, which in turn heats a water reservoir. The heated water can then be used in homes. The advantage of solar thermal is that the heated water can be stored until it is needed, eliminating the need for a separate energy storage system.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-94" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-94"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>94<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Solar thermal power can also be converted to electricity by using the steam generated from the heated water to drive a <a href="/wiki/Turbine" title="Turbine">turbine</a> connected to a generator. However, because generating electricity this way is much more expensive than photovoltaic power plants, there are very few in use today.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-95" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-95"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>95<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Floatovoltaics">Floatovoltaics</h4><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=12" title="Edit section: Floatovoltaics"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Floating_solar" title="Floating solar">Floating solar</a></div>
<p>Floatovoltiacs, or floating solar panels, are solar panels floating on bodies of water. There are both positive and negative points to this. Some positive points are increased efficiency and price decrease of water space compared to land space. A negative point is that making floating solar panels could be more expensive.
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Agrivoltaics">Agrivoltaics</h4><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=13" title="Edit section: Agrivoltaics"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Agrivoltaics" title="Agrivoltaics">Agrivoltaics</a></div>
<p>Agrivoltaics is where there is simultaneous use of land for energy production and agriculture. There are again both positive and negative points. A positive viewpoint is there is a better use of land, which leads to lower land costs. A negative viewpoint is it the plants grown underneath would have to be plants that can grow well under shade, such as <a href="/wiki/Polka_Dot_Plant" class="mw-redirect" title="Polka Dot Plant">Polka Dot Plant</a>, <a href="/wiki/Pineapple_Sage" class="mw-redirect" title="Pineapple Sage">Pineapple Sage</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Begonia" title="Begonia">Begonia</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-96" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-96"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>96<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Agrivoltaics not only optimizes land use and reduces costs by enabling dual revenue streams from both energy production and agriculture, but it can also help moderate temperatures beneath the panels, potentially reducing water loss and improving <a href="/wiki/Microclimate" title="Microclimate">microclimates</a> for crop growth. However, careful design and crop selection are crucial, as the shading effect may limit the types of plants that can thrive, necessitating the use of shade-tolerant species and innovative management practices.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-97" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-97"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>97<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Wind_power">Wind power</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=14" title="Edit section: Wind power"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main articles: <a href="/wiki/Wind_power" title="Wind power">Wind power</a> and <a href="/wiki/Wind_power_by_country" title="Wind power by country">Wind power by country</a></div>
<figure typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Wind_energy_generation_by_region,_OWID.svg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/Wind_energy_generation_by_region%2C_OWID.svg/330px-Wind_energy_generation_by_region%2C_OWID.svg.png" decoding="async" width="300" height="212" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/Wind_energy_generation_by_region%2C_OWID.svg/960px-Wind_energy_generation_by_region%2C_OWID.svg.png 2x" data-file-width="850" data-file-height="600" /></a><figcaption>Wind energy generation by region over time<sup id="cite&#95;ref-98" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-98"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>98<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></figcaption></figure>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Pretty_flamingos_-_geograph.org.uk_-_578705.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Pretty_flamingos_-_geograph.org.uk_-_578705.jpg/250px-Pretty_flamingos_-_geograph.org.uk_-_578705.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="339" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/8/83/Pretty_flamingos_-_geograph.org.uk_-_578705.jpg 2x" data-file-width="472" data-file-height="640" /></a><figcaption><a href="/wiki/Burbo_Bank_Offshore_Wind_Farm" title="Burbo Bank Offshore Wind Farm">Burbo</a>, <a href="/wiki/North_West_England" title="North West England">NW-England</a></figcaption></figure>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Fentonwindpark1.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/Fentonwindpark1.jpg/250px-Fentonwindpark1.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="188" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/Fentonwindpark1.jpg/500px-Fentonwindpark1.jpg 2x" data-file-width="2560" data-file-height="1920" /></a><figcaption>Sunrise at the <a href="/wiki/Fenton_Wind_Farm" title="Fenton Wind Farm">Fenton Wind Farm</a> in Minnesota, United States</figcaption></figure>

</td></tr>
<tr>
<td>Primary technology</td>
<td><a href="/wiki/Wind_turbine" title="Wind turbine">Wind turbine</a>, <a href="/wiki/Windmill" title="Windmill">windmill</a>
</td></tr>
<tr>
<td>Main applications</td>
<td>Electricity, pumping water (<a href="/wiki/Windpump" title="Windpump">windpump</a>)
</td></tr></tbody></table><p>Humans have harnessed wind energy since at least 3500 BC. Until the 20th century, it was primarily used to power ships, windmills and water pumps. Today, the vast majority of wind power is used to generate electricity using wind turbines.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:2&#95;12-6" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:2-12"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>12<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Modern utility-scale wind turbines range from around 600&#160;kW to 9 MW of rated power. The power available from the wind is a function of the cube of the wind speed, so as wind speed increases, power output increases up to the maximum output for the particular turbine.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-EWEA&#95;102-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-EWEA-102"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>102<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Areas where winds are stronger and more constant, such as <a href="/wiki/Offshore_wind_power" title="Offshore wind power">offshore</a> and high-altitude sites, are preferred locations for wind farms.
</p><p>Wind-generated electricity met nearly 4% of global electricity demand in 2015, with nearly 63&#160;GW of new wind power capacity installed. Wind energy was the leading source of new capacity in Europe, the US and Canada, and the second largest in China. In Denmark, wind energy met more than 40% of its electricity demand while Ireland, Portugal and <a href="/wiki/Renewable_energy_in_Spain" title="Renewable energy in Spain">Spain</a> each met nearly 20%.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-103" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-103"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>103<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Globally, the long-term technical potential of wind energy is believed to be five times total current global energy production, or 40 times current electricity demand, assuming all practical barriers needed were overcome. This would require wind turbines to be installed over large areas, particularly in areas of higher wind resources, such as offshore, and likely also industrial use of new types of <a href="/wiki/VAWT" class="mw-redirect" title="VAWT">VAWT</a> turbines in addition to the horizontal axis units currently in use. As offshore wind speeds average ~90% greater than that of land, offshore resources can contribute substantially more energy than land-stationed turbines.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-104" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-104"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>104<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Investments in wind technologies reached USD 161 billion in 2020, with onshore wind dominating at 80% of total investments from 2013 to 2022. Offshore wind investments nearly doubled to USD 41 billion between 2019 and 2020, primarily due to policy incentives in China and expansion in Europe. Global wind capacity increased by 557 GW between 2013 and 2021, with capacity additions increasing by an average of 19% each year.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:1&#95;67-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:1-67"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>67<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Hydropower">Hydropower</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=15" title="Edit section: Hydropower"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main articles: <a href="/wiki/Hydroelectricity" title="Hydroelectricity">Hydroelectricity</a> and <a href="/wiki/Hydropower" title="Hydropower">Hydropower</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:ThreeGorgesDam-China2009.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/ThreeGorgesDam-China2009.jpg/250px-ThreeGorgesDam-China2009.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="155" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/ThreeGorgesDam-China2009.jpg/500px-ThreeGorgesDam-China2009.jpg 2x" data-file-width="2847" data-file-height="1768" /></a><figcaption>The <a href="/wiki/Three_Gorges_Dam" title="Three Gorges Dam">Three Gorges Dam</a> for <a href="/wiki/Hydropower" title="Hydropower">hydropower</a> on the <a href="/wiki/Yangtze_River" class="mw-redirect" title="Yangtze River">Yangtze River</a> in China</figcaption></figure>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Three_gorges_dam_from_space.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Three_gorges_dam_from_space.jpg/250px-Three_gorges_dam_from_space.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="153" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Three_gorges_dam_from_space.jpg/500px-Three_gorges_dam_from_space.jpg 2x" data-file-width="3924" data-file-height="2394" /></a><figcaption><a href="/wiki/Three_Gorges_Dam" title="Three Gorges Dam">Three Gorges Dam</a> and <a href="/wiki/Gezhouba_Dam" title="Gezhouba Dam">Gezhouba Dam</a>, China</figcaption></figure>
<table class="wikitable">
<tbody><tr>
<th>Installed capacity and other key design parameters

</td></tr>
