<td>1.9% (2014-2023)<sup id="cite&#95;ref-106" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-106"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>106<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</td></tr>
<tr>
<td>Share of global electricity generation</td>
<td>14.3% (2023)<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Ember&#95;2024&#95;60-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Ember_2024-60"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>60<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</td></tr>
<tr>
<td>Levelized cost per megawatt hour</td>
<td>USD 65.581 (2019)<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTENREL&#95;ATB2021Hydropower&#95;107-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTENREL_ATB2021Hydropower-107"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>107<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</td></tr>
<tr>
<td>Primary technology</td>
<td><a href="/wiki/Hydroelectricity#Generating_methods" title="Hydroelectricity">Dam</a>
</td></tr>
<tr>
<td>Main applications</td>
<td>Electricity, <a href="/wiki/Pumped-storage_hydroelectricity" title="Pumped-storage hydroelectricity">pumped storage</a>, <a href="/wiki/Hydropower#Mechanical_power" title="Hydropower">mechanical power</a>
</td></tr></tbody></table>
<p>Since water is about 800 times <a href="/wiki/Density_of_air" title="Density of air">denser than air</a>, even a slow flowing stream of water, or moderate sea <a href="/wiki/Swell_(ocean)" class="mw-redirect" title="Swell (ocean)">swell</a>, can yield considerable amounts of energy. Water can generate electricity with a <a href="/wiki/Energy_conversion_efficiency" title="Energy conversion efficiency">conversion efficiency</a> of about 90%, which is the highest rate in renewable energy.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Ang&#95;108-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Ang-108"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>108<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> There are many forms of water energy:
</p>
<ul><li>Historically, hydroelectric power came from constructing large hydroelectric dams and reservoirs, which are still popular in <a href="/wiki/Developing_country" title="Developing country">developing countries</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-109" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-109"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>109<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The <a href="/wiki/List_of_largest_hydroelectric_power_stations" title="List of largest hydroelectric power stations">largest</a> of them are the <a href="/wiki/Three_Gorges_Dam" title="Three Gorges Dam">Three Gorges Dam</a> (2003) in <a href="/wiki/China" title="China">China</a> and the <a href="/wiki/Itaipu_Dam" title="Itaipu Dam">Itaipu Dam</a> (1984) built by Brazil and Paraguay.</li>
<li><a href="/wiki/Small_hydro" title="Small hydro">Small hydro</a> systems are hydroelectric power installations that typically produce up to <span class="nowrap">50 MW</span> of power. They are often used on small rivers or as a low-impact development on larger rivers. China is the largest producer of hydroelectricity in the world and has more than 45,000 small hydro installations.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-110" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-110"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>110<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></li>
<li><a href="/wiki/Run-of-the-river_hydroelectricity" title="Run-of-the-river hydroelectricity">Run-of-the-river hydroelectricity</a> plants derive energy from rivers without the creation of a large <a href="/wiki/Reservoir" title="Reservoir">reservoir</a>. The water is typically conveyed along the side of the river valley (using channels, pipes or tunnels) until it is high above the valley floor, whereupon it can be allowed to fall through a penstock to drive a turbine. A run-of-river plant may still produce a large amount of electricity, such as the <a href="/wiki/Chief_Joseph_Dam" title="Chief Joseph Dam">Chief Joseph Dam</a> on the <a href="/wiki/Columbia_River" title="Columbia River">Columbia River</a> in the United States.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-111" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-111"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>111<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> However many run-of-the-river hydro power plants are <a href="/wiki/Micro_hydro" title="Micro hydro">micro hydro</a> or <a href="/wiki/Pico_hydro" title="Pico hydro">pico hydro</a> plants.</li></ul>
<p>Much hydropower is flexible, thus complementing wind and solar, as it not intermittent.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-112" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-112"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>112<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In 2021, the world renewable hydropower capacity was 1,360 GW.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-IEA-3&#95;88-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-IEA-3-88"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>88<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Only a third of the world's estimated hydroelectric potential of 14,000 TWh/year has been developed.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-iha2021&#95;113-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-iha2021-113"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>113<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-etp2006&#95;114-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-etp2006-114"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>114<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> New hydropower projects face opposition from local communities due to their large impact, including relocation of communities and flooding of wildlife habitats and farming land.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-115" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-115"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>115<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> High cost and lead times from permission process, including environmental and risk assessments, with lack of environmental and social acceptance are therefore the primary challenges for new developments.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-116" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-116"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>116<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> It is popular to repower old dams thereby increasing their efficiency and capacity as well as quicker responsiveness on the grid.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-117" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-117"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>117<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Where circumstances permit existing dams such as the <a href="/wiki/Richard_B._Russell_Dam" title="Richard B. Russell Dam">Russell Dam</a> built in 1985 may be updated with "pump back" facilities for <a href="/wiki/Pumped-storage_hydroelectricity" title="Pumped-storage hydroelectricity">pumped-storage</a> which is useful for peak loads or to support intermittent wind and solar power. Because <a href="/wiki/Dispatchable_generation" title="Dispatchable generation">dispatchable power</a> is more valuable than VRE<sup id="cite&#95;ref-118" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-118"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>118<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-119" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-119"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>119<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> countries with large hydroelectric developments such as Canada and Norway are spending billions to expand their grids to trade with neighboring countries having limited hydro.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-120" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-120"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>120<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Bioenergy">Bioenergy</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=16" title="Edit section: Bioenergy"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Bioenergy" title="Bioenergy">Bioenergy</a></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Further information: <a href="/wiki/Biomass_(energy)#Environmental_impacts" title="Biomass (energy)">Biomass (energy) §&#160;Environmental impacts</a></div>
<table class="wikitable">
<tbody><tr>
<th>Installed capacity and other key design parameters
</th>
<th>Value and year
</th></tr>
<tr>
<td>Global electricity generation capacity</td>
<td>150.3 GW (2023)<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEIRENA202430&#95;121-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIRENA202430-121"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>121<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</td></tr>
<tr>
<td>Global electricity generation capacity annual growth rate</td>
<td>5.8% (2014-2023)<sup id="cite&#95;ref-122" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-122"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>122<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</td></tr>
<tr>
<td>Share of global electricity generation</td>
<td>2.4% (2022)<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Ember&#95;2024&#95;60-3" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Ember_2024-60"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>60<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</td></tr>
<tr>
<td>Levelized cost per megawatt hour</td>
<td>USD 118.908 (2019)<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTENREL&#95;ATB2021Other&#95;Technologies&#95;(EIA)&#95;123-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTENREL_ATB2021Other_Technologies_(EIA)-123"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>123<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</td></tr>
<tr>
<td>Primary technologies</td>
<td><a href="/wiki/Biomass_(energy)" title="Biomass (energy)">Biomass</a>, <a href="/wiki/Biofuel" title="Biofuel">biofuel</a>
</td></tr>
<tr>
<td>Main applications</td>
<td>Electricity, heating, cooking, transportation fuels
</td></tr></tbody></table>
<p><a href="/wiki/Biomass_(energy)" title="Biomass (energy)">Biomass</a> is biological material derived from living, or recently living organisms. Most commonly, it refers to plants or plant-derived materials. As an energy source, biomass can either be used directly via <a href="/wiki/Combustion" title="Combustion">combustion</a> to produce heat, or converted to a more energy-dense <a href="/wiki/Biofuel" title="Biofuel">biofuel</a> like ethanol. Wood is the most significant biomass energy source as of 2012<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Scheck2012&#95;124-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Scheck2012-124"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>124<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and is usually sourced from a trees cleared for <a href="/wiki/Silviculture" title="Silviculture">silvicultural</a> reasons or <a href="/wiki/Fire_prevention" title="Fire prevention">fire prevention</a>. Municipal wood waste – for instance, construction materials or sawdust – is also often burned for energy.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-125" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-125"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>125<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The biggest per-capita producers of wood-based bioenergy are heavily forested countries like Finland, Sweden, Estonia, Austria, and Denmark.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-126" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-126"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>126<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Bioenergy can be environmentally destructive if old-growth forests are cleared to make way for crop production. In particular, demand for palm oil to produce biodiesel has contributed to the deforestation of tropical rainforests in Brazil and Indonesia.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:10&#95;127-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:10-127"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>127<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In addition, burning biomass still produces carbon emissions, although much less than fossil fuels (39 grams of CO<sub>2</sub> per megajoule of energy, compared to 75 g/MJ for fossil fuels).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-128" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-128"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>128<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Some <a href="/wiki/Biomass_(energy)" title="Biomass (energy)">biomass</a> sources are unsustainable at current rates of exploitation (as of 2017).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-CarbonBrief1&#95;129-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-CarbonBrief1-129"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>129<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> 
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Biofuel">Biofuel</h4><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=17" title="Edit section: Biofuel"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Biofuel" title="Biofuel">Biofuel</a></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">See also: <a href="/wiki/Ethanol_fuel" title="Ethanol fuel">Ethanol fuel</a>, <a href="/wiki/Sustainable_biofuel" title="Sustainable biofuel">Sustainable biofuel</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Issues_relating_to_biofuels" title="Issues relating to biofuels">Issues relating to biofuels</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Metz_biomass_power_station.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/Metz_biomass_power_station.jpg/250px-Metz_biomass_power_station.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="244" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/Metz_biomass_power_station.jpg/500px-Metz_biomass_power_station.jpg 2x" data-file-width="531" data-file-height="519" /></a><figcaption>A <a href="/wiki/Cogeneration" title="Cogeneration">CHP power station</a> using wood to supply 30,000 households in France</figcaption></figure>
<p><a href="/wiki/Biofuel" title="Biofuel">Biofuels</a> are primarily used in transportation, providing 3.5% of the world's transport energy demand in 2022,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-130" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-130"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>130<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> up from 2.7% in 2010.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEREN21&#95;Renewables&#95;Global&#95;Status&#95;Report201113–14&#95;131-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEREN21_Renewables_Global_Status_Report201113–14-131"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>131<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Biojet" class="mw-redirect" title="Biojet">Biojet</a> is expected to be important for short-term reduction of carbon dioxide emissions from long-haul flights.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-132" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-132"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>132<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Soybeanbus.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/Soybeanbus.jpg/250px-Soybeanbus.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="161" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/5/5c/Soybeanbus.jpg 2x" data-file-width="500" data-file-height="322" /></a><figcaption>A bus fueled by <a href="/wiki/Biodiesel" title="Biodiesel">biodiesel</a></figcaption></figure>
<p>Aside from wood, the major sources of bioenergy are <a href="/wiki/Ethanol_fuel" title="Ethanol fuel">bioethanol</a> and <a href="/wiki/Biodiesel" title="Biodiesel">biodiesel</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:2&#95;12-7" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:2-12"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>12<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Bioethanol is usually produced by fermenting the sugar components of crops like <a href="/wiki/Sugarcane" title="Sugarcane">sugarcane</a> and <a href="/wiki/Maize" title="Maize">maize</a>, while biodiesel is mostly made from oils extracted from plants, such as <a href="/wiki/Soybean_oil" title="Soybean oil">soybean oil</a> and <a href="/wiki/Corn_oil" title="Corn oil">corn oil</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:9&#95;133-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:9-133"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>133<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Most of the crops used to produce bioethanol and biodiesel are grown specifically for this purpose,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-134" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-134"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>134<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> although used <a href="/wiki/Cooking_oil" title="Cooking oil">cooking oil</a> accounted for 14% of the oil used to produce biodiesel as of 2015.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:9&#95;133-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:9-133"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>133<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The biomass used to produce biofuels varies by region. Maize is the major feedstock in the United States, while sugarcane dominates in Brazil.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-135" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-135"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>135<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In the European Union, where biodiesel is more common than bioethanol, <a href="/wiki/Rapeseed_oil" title="Rapeseed oil">rapeseed oil</a> and <a href="/wiki/Palm_oil" title="Palm oil">palm oil</a> are the main feedstocks.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-136" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-136"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>136<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> China, although it produces comparatively much less biofuel, uses mostly corn and wheat.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-137" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-137"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>137<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In many countries, biofuels are either subsidized or mandated to be <a href="/wiki/Common_ethanol_fuel_mixtures" title="Common ethanol fuel mixtures">included in fuel mixtures</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:10&#95;127-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:10-127"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>127<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Faz_S_Sofia_canavial_090607_REFON.JPG" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Faz_S_Sofia_canavial_090607_REFON.JPG/250px-Faz_S_Sofia_canavial_090607_REFON.JPG" decoding="async" width="250" height="167" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Faz_S_Sofia_canavial_090607_REFON.JPG/500px-Faz_S_Sofia_canavial_090607_REFON.JPG 2x" data-file-width="3264" data-file-height="2176" /></a><figcaption><a href="/wiki/Sustainable_biofuel#Sugarcane_in_Brazil" title="Sustainable biofuel">Sugarcane plantation</a> to produce <a href="/wiki/Ethanol_fuel" title="Ethanol fuel">ethanol</a> in Brazil</figcaption></figure>
<p>There are many other sources of bioenergy that are more niche, or not yet viable at large scales. For instance, bioethanol could be <a href="/wiki/Cellulosic_ethanol" title="Cellulosic ethanol">produced from the cellulosic parts</a> of crops, rather than only the seed as is common today.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-138" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-138"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>138<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Sweet_sorghum" title="Sweet sorghum">Sweet sorghum</a> may be a promising alternative source of bioethanol, due to its tolerance of a wide range of climates.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-139" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-139"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>139<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Cow_dung" title="Cow dung">Cow dung</a> can be converted into methane.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-140" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-140"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>140<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> There is also a great deal of research involving <a href="/wiki/Algal_fuel" class="mw-redirect" title="Algal fuel">algal fuel</a>, which is attractive because algae is a non-food resource, grows around 20 times faster than most food crops, and can be grown almost anywhere.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-ZhuLiHiltunen2018&#95;141-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-ZhuLiHiltunen2018-141"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>141<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> 
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Geothermal_energy">Geothermal energy</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=18" title="Edit section: Geothermal energy"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main articles: <a href="/wiki/Geothermal_energy" title="Geothermal energy">Geothermal energy</a>, <a href="/wiki/Geothermal_power" title="Geothermal power">Geothermal power</a>, <a href="/wiki/Renewable_thermal_energy" title="Renewable thermal energy">Renewable thermal energy</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Geothermal_energy_in_the_United_States" title="Geothermal energy in the United States">Geothermal energy in the United States</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:NesjavellirPowerPlant_edit2.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/NesjavellirPowerPlant_edit2.jpg/250px-NesjavellirPowerPlant_edit2.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="167" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/NesjavellirPowerPlant_edit2.jpg/500px-NesjavellirPowerPlant_edit2.jpg 2x" data-file-width="2400" data-file-height="1600" /></a><figcaption>Steam rising from the <a href="/wiki/Nesjavellir_Geothermal_Power_Station" title="Nesjavellir Geothermal Power Station">Nesjavellir Geothermal Power Station</a> in Iceland</figcaption></figure>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:West_Ford_Flat_Geothermal_Cooling_Tower.JPG" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/West_Ford_Flat_Geothermal_Cooling_Tower.JPG/250px-West_Ford_Flat_Geothermal_Cooling_Tower.JPG" decoding="async" width="250" height="333" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/West_Ford_Flat_Geothermal_Cooling_Tower.JPG/500px-West_Ford_Flat_Geothermal_Cooling_Tower.JPG 2x" data-file-width="2136" data-file-height="2848" /></a><figcaption>Geothermal plant at <a href="/wiki/The_Geysers" title="The Geysers">The Geysers</a>, California, US</figcaption></figure>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Krafla_geothermal_power_station_wiki.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/Krafla_geothermal_power_station_wiki.jpg/250px-Krafla_geothermal_power_station_wiki.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="188" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/Krafla_geothermal_power_station_wiki.jpg/500px-Krafla_geothermal_power_station_wiki.jpg 2x" data-file-width="2048" data-file-height="1536" /></a><figcaption><a href="/wiki/Krafla_Power_Station" title="Krafla Power Station">Krafla</a>, a <a href="/wiki/Geothermal_power_station" class="mw-redirect" title="Geothermal power station">geothermal power station</a> in Iceland</figcaption></figure>
<table class="wikitable">

</td></tr>

<td>&lt;1% (2018)<sup id="cite&#95;ref-IEA&#95;Electricity&#95;2020&#95;144-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-IEA_Electricity_2020-144"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>144<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</td></tr>
<tr>
<td>Levelized cost per megawatt hour</td>
<td>USD 58.257 (2019)<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTENREL&#95;ATB2021Geothermal&#95;145-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTENREL_ATB2021Geothermal-145"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>145<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</td></tr>
<tr>
<td>Primary technologies</td>
<td>Dry steam, flash steam, and binary cycle power stations
</td></tr>
<tr>
<td>Main applications</td>
<td>Electricity, <a href="/wiki/Geothermal_heating" title="Geothermal heating">heating</a>
</td></tr></tbody></table>
<p>Geothermal energy is <a href="/wiki/Thermal_energy" title="Thermal energy">thermal energy</a> (heat) extracted from the <a href="/wiki/Earth%27s_crust" title="Earth&#39;s crust">Earth's crust</a>. It originates from several different <a href="/wiki/Earth%27s_internal_heat_budget#Sources_of_heat" title="Earth&#39;s internal heat budget">sources</a>, of which the most significant is slow <a href="/wiki/Radioactive_decay" title="Radioactive decay">radioactive decay</a> of minerals contained in the <a href="/wiki/Internal_structure_of_Earth" title="Internal structure of Earth">Earth's interior</a>,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:2&#95;12-8" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:2-12"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>12<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> as well as some leftover heat from the <a href="/wiki/Earth#Formation" title="Earth">formation of the Earth</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:11&#95;146-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:11-146"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>146<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Some of the heat is generated near the Earth's surface in the crust, but some also flows from deep within the Earth from the <a href="/wiki/Earth%27s_mantle" title="Earth&#39;s mantle">mantle</a> and <a href="/wiki/Internal_structure_of_Earth#Core" title="Internal structure of Earth">core</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:11&#95;146-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:11-146"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>146<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Geothermal energy extraction is viable mostly in countries located on <a href="/wiki/Plate_tectonics" title="Plate tectonics">tectonic plate</a> edges, where the Earth's hot mantle is more exposed.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:13&#95;147-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:13-147"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>147<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> As of 2023, the United States has by far the most geothermal capacity (2.7 GW,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:12&#95;148-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:12-148"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>148<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> or less than 0.2% of the country's total energy capacity<sup id="cite&#95;ref-149" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-149"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>149<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>), followed by Indonesia and the Philippines. Global capacity in 2022 was 15 GW.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:12&#95;148-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:12-148"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>148<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Geothermal energy can be either used directly to heat homes, as is common in Iceland where almost all of its energy is renewable, or to generate electricity. Iceland is a global leader in renewable energy, relying almost entirely on its abundant geothermal and hydroelectric resources derived from volcanic activity and glaciers.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-150" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-150"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>150<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> At smaller scales, geothermal power can be generated with <a href="/wiki/Ground_source_heat_pump" title="Ground source heat pump">geothermal heat pumps</a>, which can extract heat from ground temperatures of under 30&#160;°C (86&#160;°F), allowing them to be used at relatively shallow depths of a few meters.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:13&#95;147-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:13-147"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>147<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Electricity generation requires large plants and ground temperatures of at least 150&#160;°C (302&#160;°F). In some countries, electricity produced from geothermal energy accounts for a large portion of the total, such as Kenya (43%) and Indonesia (5%).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-151" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-151"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>151<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Technical advances may eventually make geothermal power more widely available. For example, <a href="/wiki/Enhanced_geothermal_system" title="Enhanced geothermal system">enhanced geothermal systems</a> involve drilling around 10 kilometres (6.2&#160;mi) into the Earth, breaking apart hot rocks and extracting the heat using water. In theory, this type of geothermal energy extraction could be done anywhere on Earth.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:13&#95;147-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:13-147"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>147<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Emerging_technologies">Emerging technologies</h2><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=19" title="Edit section: Emerging technologies"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>There are also other renewable energy technologies that are still under development, including <a href="/wiki/Enhanced_geothermal_system" title="Enhanced geothermal system">enhanced geothermal systems</a>, <a href="/wiki/Concentrated_solar_power" title="Concentrated solar power">concentrated solar power</a>, <a href="/wiki/Cellulosic_ethanol" title="Cellulosic ethanol">cellulosic ethanol</a>, <a href="/wiki/Piezoelectricity" title="Piezoelectricity">piezoelectricity</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Marine_energy" title="Marine energy">marine energy</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:7&#95;152-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:7-152"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>152<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-IEA&#95;153-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-IEA-153"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>153<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> These technologies are not yet widely demonstrated or have limited commercialization. Some may have potential comparable to other renewable energy technologies, but still depend on further breakthroughs from research, development and engineering.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-IEA&#95;153-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-IEA-153"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>153<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Enhanced_geothermal_systems">Enhanced geothermal systems</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=20" title="Edit section: Enhanced geothermal systems"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Enhanced_geothermal_systems" class="mw-redirect" title="Enhanced geothermal systems">Enhanced geothermal systems</a></div>
<p>Enhanced geothermal systems (EGS) are a new type of geothermal power which does not require natural hot water reservoirs or steam to generate power. Most of the underground heat within drilling reach is trapped in solid rocks, not in water.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Duchane&#95;154-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Duchane-154"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>154<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> EGS technologies use <a href="/wiki/Hydraulic_fracturing" class="mw-redirect" title="Hydraulic fracturing">hydraulic fracturing</a> to break apart these rocks and release the heat they contain, which is then harvested by pumping water into the ground. The process is sometimes known as "hot dry rock" (HDR).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-155" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-155"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>155<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Unlike conventional geothermal energy extraction, EGS may be feasible anywhere in the world, depending on the cost of drilling.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-156" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-156"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>156<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> EGS projects have so far primarily been limited to <a href="/wiki/Demonstration_plant" title="Demonstration plant">demonstration plants</a>, as the technology is capital-intensive due to the high cost of drilling.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-157" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-157"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>157<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Sand_battery">Sand battery</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=21" title="Edit section: Sand battery"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>Sand batteries are large tanks filled with soapstone that absorb heat. Excess heat energy from renewable energy is piped into the tank and then energy is discharged as boiling water, steam, or heated air. Finland is using this technology in <a href="/wiki/Pornainen" title="Pornainen">Pornainen</a> as Polar Night Energy built a 1MW sand battery that can store up to 100 MWh that went online in 2025.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-158" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-158"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>158<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-159" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-159"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>159<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Piezoelectricity">Piezoelectricity</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=22" title="Edit section: Piezoelectricity"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p><a href="/wiki/Piezoelectricity" title="Piezoelectricity">Piezoelectricity</a> is the conversion of existing <a href="/wiki/Stress_(mechanics)" title="Stress (mechanics)">mechanical stress</a> or vibration (<a href="/wiki/Classical_mechanics" title="Classical mechanics">classical mechanics</a>) into an electrical charge without consuming or depleting a fuel source.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-160" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-160"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>160<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-161" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-161"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>161<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Piezotronics" title="Piezotronics">Piezotronics</a> enables the interaction of piezoelectric and semiconducting behaviors to modulate energy barriers at contact surface, thereby controlling charge carrier transport.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-162" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-162"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>162<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Since the introduction of <a href="/wiki/Nanogenerator" title="Nanogenerator">nanogenerators</a>, the efficiency of microscale energy harvesting has improved. For instance, nanogenerators typically consist of piezoelectric <a href="/wiki/Nanowire" title="Nanowire">nanowires</a>; as these wires bend or compress, the applied mechanical stress causes the ions within the material's <a href="/wiki/Crystal_lattice" class="mw-redirect" title="Crystal lattice">crystal lattice</a> to shift their positions. This shift disrupts the nanowire's charge symmetry which causes an instantaneous charge <a href="/wiki/Polarization_(physics)" class="mw-redirect" title="Polarization (physics)">polarization</a> (separation of positive and negative charges) across the nanowire's ends. Once polarized, electrons are freed from the attached electrode which generates <a href="/wiki/Alternating_current" title="Alternating current">usable alternating current</a> (AC) electricity that can energize low-power sensors.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-163" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-163"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>163<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-164" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-164"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>164<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Piezoelectric_microelectromechanical_systems" title="Piezoelectric microelectromechanical systems">Piezoelectric microelectromechanical systems</a> (piezoMEMS), such as <a href="/wiki/Actuator" title="Actuator">actuators</a> for artificial organs and <a href="/wiki/Pacemaker" title="Pacemaker">pacemakers</a> or <a href="/wiki/Micropump" title="Micropump">micropumps</a> for drug delivery and <a href="/wiki/Reagent" title="Reagent">reagent</a> transfers, are vital for medical purposes and energy harvesting.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-165" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-165"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>165<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Furthermore, specialized components like <a href="/wiki/Piezoelectric_resonator_(disambiguation)" class="mw-redirect mw-disambig" title="Piezoelectric resonator (disambiguation)">piezoelectric resonators</a> and quartz <a href="/wiki/Crystal_oscillator" title="Crystal oscillator">crystal oscillators</a> are used to regulate electrical circuit frequencies.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-166" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-166"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>166<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Marine_energy">Marine energy</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=23" title="Edit section: Marine energy"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Marine_energy" title="Marine energy">Marine energy</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Sihwa_Lake_Tidal_Power_Station_aerial_view.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Sihwa_Lake_Tidal_Power_Station_aerial_view.jpg/250px-Sihwa_Lake_Tidal_Power_Station_aerial_view.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="167" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Sihwa_Lake_Tidal_Power_Station_aerial_view.jpg/500px-Sihwa_Lake_Tidal_Power_Station_aerial_view.jpg 2x" data-file-width="5435" data-file-height="3623" /></a><figcaption>Aerial view of Sihwa Tidal Power Station in South Korea</figcaption></figure>
<p>Marine energy (also sometimes referred to as ocean energy) is the energy carried by <a href="/wiki/Ocean_wave" class="mw-redirect" title="Ocean wave">ocean waves</a>, <a href="/wiki/Tide" title="Tide">tides</a>, <a href="/wiki/Salinity" title="Salinity">salinity</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Ocean_thermal_energy_conversion" title="Ocean thermal energy conversion">ocean temperature differences</a>. Technologies to harness the energy of moving water include <a href="/wiki/Wave_power" title="Wave power">wave power</a>, <a href="/wiki/Marine_current_power" title="Marine current power">marine current power</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Tidal_power" title="Tidal power">tidal power</a>. <a href="/wiki/Reverse_electrodialysis" class="mw-redirect" title="Reverse electrodialysis">Reverse electrodialysis</a> (RED) is a technology for generating electricity by mixing <a href="/wiki/Fresh_water" title="Fresh water">fresh water</a> and salty <a href="/wiki/Seawater" title="Seawater">sea water</a> in large power cells.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-167" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-167"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>167<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Most marine energy harvesting technologies are still at low <a href="/wiki/Technology_readiness_level" title="Technology readiness level">technology readiness levels</a> and not used at large scales. Tidal energy is generally considered the most mature, but has not seen wide deployment.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-168" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-168"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>168<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The world's largest tidal power station is on <a href="/wiki/Sihwa_Lake_Tidal_Power_Station" title="Sihwa Lake Tidal Power Station">Sihwa Lake</a>, South Korea,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-169" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-169"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>169<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> which produces around 550 gigawatt-hours of electricity per year.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-170" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-170"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>170<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Earth_infrared_thermal_radiation">Earth infrared thermal radiation</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=24" title="Edit section: Earth infrared thermal radiation"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>Earth emits roughly 10<sup>17</sup> W of infrared thermal radiation that flows toward the cold outer space. Solar energy hits the surface and atmosphere of the earth and produces heat. Using various theorized devices like emissive energy harvester (EEH) or thermoradiative diode, this energy flow can be converted into electricity. In theory, this technology can be used during nighttime.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-171" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-171"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>171<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-172" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-172"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>172<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Others">Others</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=25" title="Edit section: Others"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Algae_fuels">Algae fuels</h4><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=26" title="Edit section: Algae fuels"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Algae_fuels" class="mw-redirect" title="Algae fuels">Algae fuels</a></div>
<p>Producing liquid fuels from oil-rich (fat-rich) varieties of algae is an ongoing research topic. Various microalgae grown in open or closed systems are being tried including some systems that can be set up in brownfield and desert lands.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-173" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-173"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>173<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Space-based_solar_power">Space-based solar power</h4><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=27" title="Edit section: Space-based solar power"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Space-based_solar_power" title="Space-based solar power">Space-based solar power</a></div>
<p>There have been numerous proposals for <a href="/wiki/Space-based_solar_power" title="Space-based solar power">space-based solar power</a>, in which very large satellites with photovoltaic panels would be equipped with <a href="/wiki/Microwave" title="Microwave">microwave</a> transmitters to beam power back to terrestrial receivers. A 2024 study by the <a href="/wiki/NASA" title="NASA">NASA</a> Office of Science and Technology Policy examined the concept and concluded that with current and near-future technologies it would be economically uncompetitive.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-174" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-174"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>174<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Water_vapor">Water vapor</h4><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=28" title="Edit section: Water vapor"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>Collection of static electricity charges from water droplets on metal surfaces is an experimental technology that would be especially useful in <a href="/wiki/Developing_country" title="Developing country">low-income countries</a> with relative air humidity over 60%.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-175" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-175"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>175<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Nuclear_energy">Nuclear energy</h4><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=29" title="Edit section: Nuclear energy"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p><a href="/wiki/Breeder_reactor" title="Breeder reactor">Breeder reactors</a> could, in principle, depending on the fuel cycle employed, extract almost all of the energy contained in <a href="/wiki/Uranium" title="Uranium">uranium</a> or <a href="/wiki/Thorium" title="Thorium">thorium</a>, decreasing fuel requirements by a factor of 100 compared to widely used once-through <a href="/wiki/Light_water_reactors" class="mw-redirect" title="Light water reactors">light water reactors</a>, which extract less than 1% of the energy in the actinide metal (uranium or thorium) mined from the earth.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Argonne&#95;176-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Argonne-176"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>176<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The high fuel-efficiency of breeder reactors could greatly reduce concerns about fuel supply, energy used in mining, and storage of <a href="/wiki/Radioactive_waste" title="Radioactive waste">radioactive waste</a>. With <a href="/wiki/Seawater_uranium_extraction" class="mw-redirect" title="Seawater uranium extraction">seawater uranium extraction</a> (currently too expensive to be economical), there is enough fuel for breeder reactors to satisfy the world's energy needs for 5 billion years at 1983's total energy consumption rate, thus making nuclear energy effectively a renewable energy.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-sustainablenuclear&#95;177-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-sustainablenuclear-177"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>177<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-178" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-178"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>178<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In addition to seawater the average crustal granite rocks contain significant quantities of uranium and thorium with which breeder reactors can supply abundant energy for the remaining lifespan of the sun on the main sequence of stellar evolution.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-179" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-179"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>179<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Artificial_photosynthesis">Artificial photosynthesis</h4><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=30" title="Edit section: Artificial photosynthesis"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Artificial_photosynthesis" title="Artificial photosynthesis">Artificial photosynthesis</a></div>
<p>Artificial photosynthesis uses techniques including <a href="/wiki/Nanotechnology" title="Nanotechnology">nanotechnology</a> to store solar electromagnetic energy in chemical bonds by splitting water to produce hydrogen and then using carbon dioxide to make methanol.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-180" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-180"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>180<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Researchers in this field strived to design molecular mimics of photosynthesis that use a wider region of the solar spectrum, employ catalytic systems made from abundant, inexpensive materials that are robust, readily repaired, non-toxic, stable in a variety of environmental conditions and perform more efficiently allowing a greater proportion of photon energy to end up in the storage compounds, i.e., carbohydrates (rather than building and sustaining living cells).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-faunce&#95;181-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-faunce-181"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>181<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> However, prominent research faces hurdles, Sun Catalytix a MIT spin-off stopped scaling up their prototype fuel-cell in 2012 because it offers few savings over other ways to make hydrogen from sunlight.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-182" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-182"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>182<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> 
</p><p>Recent research emphasizes that while artificial photosynthesis shows promise in splitting water to generate hydrogen, its broader significance lies in the ability to produce dense, carbon-based solar fuels suitable for transport applications, such as aviation and long-haul shipping. These fuels, if derived from carbon dioxide and water using sunlight, could close the carbon loop and reduce reliance on fossil-based hydrocarbons. However, realizing this potential requires overcoming major technical hurdles, including the development of efficient, durable catalysts for water oxidation and CO<sub>2</sub> reduction, and careful attention to land use and public perception.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-183" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-183"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>183<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p><br />
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Comparison_of_the_theoretical_and_practical_potentials_of_different_renewable_energy_technologies">Comparison of the theoretical and practical potentials of different renewable energy technologies</h2><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=31" title="Edit section: Comparison of the theoretical and practical potentials of different renewable energy technologies"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>Global energy consumption in 2019 was approximately 65 petawatt-hours (PWh) per year or 65,000 terawatt-hours (TWh) annually.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-184" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-184"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>184<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Technical_Potential_by_Technology">Technical Potential by Technology</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=32" title="Edit section: Technical Potential by Technology"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>According to a comprehensive peer-reviewed study published in 2023,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-185" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-185"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>185<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> the technical potentials for utility-scale solar photovoltaic, concentrated solar power, onshore wind, and offshore wind each exceed 100 PWh/year, and thus each of them is capable of meeting the Humankind's total demand <i>in theory</i>.
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Solar_Photovoltaic_(PV)"><span id="Solar_Photovoltaic_.28PV.29"></span>Solar Photovoltaic (PV)</h4><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=33" title="Edit section: Solar Photovoltaic (PV)"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>Technical Potential: Over 5,800 PWh per year from solar PV alone using current technology.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-186" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-186"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>186<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> This represents approximately 89 times current global energy demand. Thus, utilizing only 2% of the solar power can solve Humankind's energy problems, in principle.
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Wind_Energy_(Onshore_+_Offshore)"><span id="Wind_Energy_.28Onshore_.2B_Offshore.29"></span>Wind Energy (Onshore + Offshore)</h4><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=34" title="Edit section: Wind Energy (Onshore + Offshore)"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>Technical Potential: Nearly 900 PWh per year from onshore and offshore wind combined, in kilowatts: 900,000,000,000,000 kWh/year (900 trillion kWh/year).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-187" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-187"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>187<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
This represents approximately 14 times current global energy demand. However, it is highly intermittent, and it is not feasible to cover most of the earth surface to collect all wind.
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Combined_Solar_and_Wind">Combined Solar and Wind</h4><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=35" title="Edit section: Combined Solar and Wind"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>With current technology, at least 6,700 PWh per year can be captured from solar and wind, which is more than 100 times global energy demand.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-188" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-188"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>188<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Hydropower_2">Hydropower</h4><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=36" title="Edit section: Hydropower"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>Gross  Theoretical Potential: Approximately 52 PWh/year distributed over 11.8  million potential locations worldwide.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-189" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-189"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>189<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
In  kilowatts: 52,000,000,000,000 kWh/year (52 trillion kWh/year). 
This  equals to about 33% of annually required energy.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-190" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-190"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>190<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Geothermal_Energy_2">Geothermal Energy</h4><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=37" title="Edit section: Geothermal Energy"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>Conventional     Geothermal: Technical potential above 10 PWh/year.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-191" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-191"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>191<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
Enhanced     Geothermal Systems (EGS): About 600 terawatts (TW) of geothermal     capacity using thermal resources within 8 km of depth, equivalent to about     4,000 PWh of technical potential for annual generation.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-192" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-192"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>192<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
The     full technical potential of next-generation geothermal systems is     sufficient to meet global electricity demand 140 times over and is second     only to solar PV among renewable technologies.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-193" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-193"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>193<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>     
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Other_Ocean_Technologies">Other Ocean Technologies</h4><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=38" title="Edit section: Other Ocean Technologies"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>Rooftop     Solar PV, Wave, and Tidal: Technical potentials above 1 PWh/year each.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-194" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-194"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>194<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
Ocean     Thermal Energy Conversion (OTEC): Technical potential above 10     PWh/year.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-195" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-195"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>195<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
Salinity     Gradient: Technical potential above 0.1 PWh/year.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-196" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-196"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>196<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Overall_Technical_Potential">Overall Technical Potential</h4><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=39" title="Edit section: Overall Technical Potential"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>When including all renewable energy sources, the global technical potential ranges from 164 to 27,200 PWh/year, which is 6.6 to 1,101 times the 2021 electricity consumption of 24.7 PWh/year.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-197" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-197"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>197<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Economic_Potential">Economic Potential</h4><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=40" title="Edit section: Economic Potential"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>The literature assessing the global economic potential of renewables shows that the economic potential is higher than current and near-future electricity demand.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-198" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-198"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>198<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> More specifically:
Around 60% of the world's solar resource and 15% of its wind resource is already economically competitive compared with local fossil fuel generation.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-199" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-199"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>199<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Market_and_industry_trends">Market and industry trends<span class="anchor" id="Economic&#95;trends"></span></h2><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=41" title="Edit section: Market and industry trends"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Renewable_energy_commercialization" title="Renewable energy commercialization">Renewable energy commercialization</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:1985-_Electricity_generation_from_renewables,_percentages_by_country.svg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/1985-_Electricity_generation_from_renewables%2C_percentages_by_country.svg/250px-1985-_Electricity_generation_from_renewables%2C_percentages_by_country.svg.png" decoding="async" width="250" height="141" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/1985-_Electricity_generation_from_renewables%2C_percentages_by_country.svg/500px-1985-_Electricity_generation_from_renewables%2C_percentages_by_country.svg.png 2x" data-file-width="1200" data-file-height="675" /></a><figcaption>Though the share of electricity generation from renewables has increased since 2010, there is substantial variance among major world regions.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-EmberStatisticalReviewFor2025&#95;200-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-EmberStatisticalReviewFor2025-200"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>200<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></figcaption></figure>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:2024-2025_Change_in_electricity_sources_and_demand.svg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/2024-2025_Change_in_electricity_sources_and_demand.svg/250px-2024-2025_Change_in_electricity_sources_and_demand.svg.png" decoding="async" width="250" height="188" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/2024-2025_Change_in_electricity_sources_and_demand.svg/500px-2024-2025_Change_in_electricity_sources_and_demand.svg.png 2x" data-file-width="1000" data-file-height="750" /></a><figcaption>Growth in solar and wind power from the first half of 2024 to the first half of 2025 increased more than the growth in overall demand for electricity, reducing reliance on fossil fuels and helping to curb <a href="/wiki/Greenhouse_gas_emissions" title="Greenhouse gas emissions">greenhouse gas emissions</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Ember&#95;20251007&#95;201-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Ember_20251007-201"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>201<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></figcaption></figure>
<p>Most new renewables are solar, followed by wind then hydro then bioenergy.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-202" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-202"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>202<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Investment in renewables, especially solar, tends to be more effective in creating jobs than coal, gas or oil.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-203" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-203"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>203<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-204" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-204"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>204<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Worldwide, renewables employ about 12 million people as of 2020, with solar PV being the technology employing the most at almost 4 million.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-205" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-205"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>205<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> However, as of February 2024, the world's supply of workforce for solar energy is lagging greatly behind demand as universities worldwide still produce more workforce for fossil fuels than for renewable energy industries.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-206" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-206"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>206<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>In 2021, China accounted for almost half of the global increase in renewable electricity.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-207" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-207"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>207<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> There were 3,146 gigawatts installed in 135 countries, while 156 countries have laws regulating the renewable energy sector.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:8&#95;7-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:8-7"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>7<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEREN21&#95;Renewables&#95;Global&#95;Status&#95;Report2021&#95;208-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEREN21_Renewables_Global_Status_Report2021-208"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>208<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The <a href="/wiki/International_Renewable_Energy_Agency" title="International Renewable Energy Agency">International Renewable Energy Agency</a> reported that during 2025 renewables accounted for 85.6% of new electricity generation capacity globally, with <a href="/wiki/Solar_photovoltaics" class="mw-redirect" title="Solar photovoltaics">solar photovoltaics</a> providing nearly three-quarters of the increase. At the end of 2025, renewables constituted 49.4% of global installed electricity generating capacity.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-thereg-20260401&#95;209-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-thereg-20260401-209"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>209<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-irena-202603&#95;210-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-irena-202603-210"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>210<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Globally in 2020 there are over 10 million jobs associated with the renewable energy industries, with solar photovoltaics being the largest renewable employer.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-211" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-211"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>211<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The clean energy sectors added about 4.7 million jobs globally between 2019 and 2022, totaling 35 million jobs by 2022.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-212" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-212"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>212<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page: 5">&#58;&#8202;5&#8202;</span></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Usage_by_sector_or_application">Usage by sector or application</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=42" title="Edit section: Usage by sector or application"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>Some studies say that a global transition to <a href="/wiki/100%25_renewable_energy" title="100% renewable energy">100% renewable energy</a> across all sectors – power, heat, transport and industry – is feasible and economically viable.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Bogdanov-2021&#95;213-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Bogdanov-2021-213"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>213<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-Teske-2019&#95;214-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Teske-2019-214"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>214<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-215" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-215"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>215<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>One of the efforts to decarbonize transportation is the increased use of <a href="/wiki/Electric_vehicle" title="Electric vehicle">electric vehicles</a> (EVs).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-wg3&#95;216-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-wg3-216"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>216<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Despite that and the use of <a href="/wiki/Biofuel" title="Biofuel">biofuels</a>, such as <a href="/wiki/Aviation_biofuel" title="Aviation biofuel">biojet</a>, less than 4% of transport energy is from renewables.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Ren21b&#95;217-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Ren21b-217"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>217<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Occasionally <a href="/wiki/Hydrogen_fuel_cells" class="mw-redirect" title="Hydrogen fuel cells">hydrogen fuel cells</a> are used for heavy transport.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-218" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-218"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>218<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Meanwhile, in the future <a href="/wiki/Electrofuels" class="mw-redirect" title="Electrofuels">electrofuels</a> may also play a greater role in decarbonizing hard-to-abate sectors like aviation and maritime shipping.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-219" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-219"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>219<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p><a href="/wiki/Solar_water_heating" title="Solar water heating">Solar water heating</a> makes an important contribution to <a href="/wiki/Renewable_heat" title="Renewable heat">renewable heat</a> in many countries, most notably in China, which now has 70% of the global total (180&#160;GWth). Most of these systems are installed on multi-family apartment buildings<sup id="cite&#95;ref-220" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-220"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>220<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and meet a portion of the hot water needs of an estimated 50–60 million households in China. Worldwide, total installed solar water heating systems meet a portion of the water heating needs of over 70 million households.
</p><p><a href="/wiki/Heat_pumps" class="mw-redirect" title="Heat pumps">Heat pumps</a> provide both heating and cooling, and also flatten the electric demand curve and are thus an increasing priority.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-energy.gov&#95;221-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-energy.gov-221"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>221<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Renewable_thermal_energy" title="Renewable thermal energy">Renewable thermal energy</a> is also growing rapidly.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-222" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-222"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>222<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> About 10% of heating and cooling energy is from renewables.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Ren21a&#95;223-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Ren21a-223"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>223<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Cost_comparison">Cost comparison</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=43" title="Edit section: Cost comparison"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>The <a href="/wiki/International_Renewable_Energy_Agency" title="International Renewable Energy Agency">International Renewable Energy Agency</a> (IRENA) stated that ~86% (187 GW) of renewable capacity added in 2022 had lower costs than electricity generated from fossil fuels.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-IRENA&#95;20230829&#95;224-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-IRENA_20230829-224"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>224<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> IRENA also stated that capacity added since 2000 reduced electricity bills in 2022 by at least $520 billion, and that in non-OECD countries, the lifetime savings of 2022 capacity additions will reduce costs by up to $580 billion.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-IRENA&#95;20230829&#95;224-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-IRENA_20230829-224"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>224<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
