<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Growth_of_renewables">Growth of renewables</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=44" title="Edit section: Growth of renewables"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1273380762/mw-parser-output/.tmulti" /><div class="thumb tmulti tnone center"><div class="thumbinner multiimageinner" style="width:592px;max-width:592px"><div class="trow"><div class="theader">Investment and sources</div></div><div class="trow"><div class="tsingle" style="width:216px;max-width:216px"><div class="thumbimage" style="height:117px;overflow:hidden"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:20210119_Renewable_energy_investment_-_2004-_BloombergNEF.svg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/20210119_Renewable_energy_investment_-_2004-_BloombergNEF.svg/250px-20210119_Renewable_energy_investment_-_2004-_BloombergNEF.svg.png" decoding="async" width="214" height="117" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/20210119_Renewable_energy_investment_-_2004-_BloombergNEF.svg/500px-20210119_Renewable_energy_investment_-_2004-_BloombergNEF.svg.png 2x" data-file-width="1230" data-file-height="675" /></a></span></div><div class="thumbcaption">Investment: Companies, governments and households have committed increasing amounts to decarbonization, including renewable energy (solar, wind), electric vehicles and associated charging infrastructure, energy storage, energy-efficient heating systems, carbon capture and storage, and hydrogen.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-BloombergNEF&#95;20210119&#95;229-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-BloombergNEF_20210119-229"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>229<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-BloombergNEF&#95;20230126&#95;230-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-BloombergNEF_20230126-230"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>230<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-BNEF&#95;20240130&#95;231-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-BNEF_20240130-231"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>231<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-BloombergNEF&#95;20250131&#95;232-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-BloombergNEF_20250131-232"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>232<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-BloombergNEF&#95;20260126&#95;233-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-BloombergNEF_20260126-233"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>233<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></div></div><div class="tsingle" style="width:211px;max-width:211px"><div class="thumbimage" style="height:117px;overflow:hidden"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:2015-_Investment_in_clean_energy_-_IEA.svg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/2015-_Investment_in_clean_energy_-_IEA.svg/250px-2015-_Investment_in_clean_energy_-_IEA.svg.png" decoding="async" width="209" height="118" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/2015-_Investment_in_clean_energy_-_IEA.svg/500px-2015-_Investment_in_clean_energy_-_IEA.svg.png 2x" data-file-width="1200" data-file-height="675" /></a></span></div><div class="thumbcaption">By 2025, investment in the energy transition had grown to about twice that for <a href="/wiki/Fossil_fuel" title="Fossil fuel">fossil fuels</a> (oil, natural gas and coal).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-IEAworldEnergyInvestment&#95;2025&#95;234-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-IEAworldEnergyInvestment_2025-234"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>234<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></div></div><div class="tsingle" style="width:159px;max-width:159px"><div class="thumbimage" style="height:117px;overflow:hidden"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:20211104_Percentage_of_electricity_from_fossil_fuels,_nuclear,_renewables_-_biggest_fossil_fuel_emitters.svg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/20211104_Percentage_of_electricity_from_fossil_fuels%2C_nuclear%2C_renewables_-_biggest_fossil_fuel_emitters.svg/250px-20211104_Percentage_of_electricity_from_fossil_fuels%2C_nuclear%2C_renewables_-_biggest_fossil_fuel_emitters.svg.png" decoding="async" width="157" height="118" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/20211104_Percentage_of_electricity_from_fossil_fuels%2C_nuclear%2C_renewables_-_biggest_fossil_fuel_emitters.svg/330px-20211104_Percentage_of_electricity_from_fossil_fuels%2C_nuclear%2C_renewables_-_biggest_fossil_fuel_emitters.svg.png 2x" data-file-width="1000" data-file-height="750" /></a></span></div><div class="thumbcaption">The countries most reliant on fossil fuels for electricity vary widely on how great a portion of that electricity is generated from renewables, leaving wide variation in renewables' growth potential.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-BP-Ember&#95;20211103&#95;235-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-BP-Ember_20211103-235"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>235<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></div></div></div></div></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1273380762/mw-parser-output/.tmulti" /><div class="thumb tmulti tnone center"><div class="thumbinner multiimageinner" style="width:592px;max-width:592px"><div class="trow"><div class="theader">Costs</div></div><div class="trow"><div class="tsingle" style="width:234px;max-width:234px"><div class="thumbimage" style="height:130px;overflow:hidden"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:20201019_Levelized_Cost_of_Energy_(LCOE,_Lazard)_-_renewable_energy.svg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/20201019_Levelized_Cost_of_Energy_%28LCOE%2C_Lazard%29_-_renewable_energy.svg/250px-20201019_Levelized_Cost_of_Energy_%28LCOE%2C_Lazard%29_-_renewable_energy.svg.png" decoding="async" width="232" height="131" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/20201019_Levelized_Cost_of_Energy_%28LCOE%2C_Lazard%29_-_renewable_energy.svg/500px-20201019_Levelized_Cost_of_Energy_%28LCOE%2C_Lazard%29_-_renewable_energy.svg.png 2x" data-file-width="1200" data-file-height="675" /></a></span></div><div class="thumbcaption"><b>Levelized cost:</b> With increasingly widespread implementation of renewable energy sources, costs have declined, most notably for energy generated by solar panels.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-LazardPopSci&#95;costs&#95;236-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-LazardPopSci_costs-236"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>236<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-LazardLCOE&#95;2024&#95;237-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-LazardLCOE_2024-237"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>237<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
<br /><a href="/wiki/Levelized_cost_of_energy" class="mw-redirect" title="Levelized cost of energy">Levelized cost of energy</a> (LCOE) is a measure of the average net present cost of electricity generation for a generating plant over its lifetime.</div></div><div class="tsingle" style="width:176px;max-width:176px"><div class="thumbimage" style="height:130px;overflow:hidden"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:2010-_Cost_of_renewable_energy_-_IRENA.svg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/2010-_Cost_of_renewable_energy_-_IRENA.svg/250px-2010-_Cost_of_renewable_energy_-_IRENA.svg.png" decoding="async" width="174" height="131" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/2010-_Cost_of_renewable_energy_-_IRENA.svg/500px-2010-_Cost_of_renewable_energy_-_IRENA.svg.png 2x" data-file-width="1000" data-file-height="750" /></a></span></div><div class="thumbcaption"><b>Past costs</b> of producing renewable energy declined significantly,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-IRENArenewableCostsFor&#95;2022&#95;238-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-IRENArenewableCostsFor_2022-238"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>238<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> with 62% of total renewable power generation added in 2020 having lower costs than the cheapest new fossil fuel option.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-IRENA&#95;20210622&#95;239-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-IRENA_20210622-239"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>239<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></div></div><div class="tsingle" style="width:176px;max-width:176px"><div class="thumbimage" style="height:130px;overflow:hidden"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:2010-_Decreasing_renewable_energy_costs_versus_deployment.svg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/2010-_Decreasing_renewable_energy_costs_versus_deployment.svg/250px-2010-_Decreasing_renewable_energy_costs_versus_deployment.svg.png" decoding="async" width="174" height="131" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/2010-_Decreasing_renewable_energy_costs_versus_deployment.svg/500px-2010-_Decreasing_renewable_energy_costs_versus_deployment.svg.png 2x" data-file-width="1000" data-file-height="750" /></a></span></div><div class="thumbcaption">"<b>Learning curves</b>": Trend of costs and deployment over time, with steeper lines showing greater cost reductions as deployment progresses.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-240" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-240"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>240<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> With increased deployment, renewables benefit from <a href="/wiki/Learning_curves" class="mw-redirect" title="Learning curves">learning curves</a> and <a href="/wiki/Economies_of_scale" title="Economies of scale">economies of scale</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-241" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-241"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>241<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></div></div></div></div></div>
<div style="clear:both;" class=""></div>
<p>The results of a recent review of the literature concluded that as <a href="/wiki/Greenhouse_gas" title="Greenhouse gas">greenhouse gas</a> (GHG) emitters begin to be held liable for damages resulting from GHG emissions resulting in climate change, a high value for liability mitigation would provide powerful incentives for deployment of renewable energy technologies.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-242" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-242"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>242<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>In the decade of 2010–2019, worldwide investment in renewable energy capacity excluding large hydropower amounted to US$2.7 trillion, of which the top countries China contributed US$818 billion, the United States contributed US$392.3 billion, Japan contributed US$210.9 billion, Germany contributed US$183.4 billion, and the United Kingdom contributed US$126.5 billion.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-2020gt-c4d&#95;243-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-2020gt-c4d-243"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>243<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> This was an increase of over three and possibly four times the equivalent amount invested in the decade of 2000–2009 (no data is available for 2000–2003).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-2020gt-c4d&#95;243-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-2020gt-c4d-243"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>243<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>As of 2022, an estimated 28% of the world's electricity was generated by renewables. This is up from 19% in 1990.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-244" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-244"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>244<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> By the end of 2024, global renewable power capacity reached 4,300 gigawatts (GW), with solar photovoltaics accounting for over 60% of annual additions.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-245" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-245"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>245<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Future_projections">Future projections</h4><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=45" title="Edit section: Future projections"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">See also: <a href="/wiki/Energy_transition" title="Energy transition">Energy transition</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:2010-_Fossil_fuels_vs_Wind_%2B_Solar_-_electricity_generation.svg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/2010-_Fossil_fuels_vs_Wind_%2B_Solar_-_electricity_generation.svg/250px-2010-_Fossil_fuels_vs_Wind_%2B_Solar_-_electricity_generation.svg.png" decoding="async" width="250" height="218" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/2010-_Fossil_fuels_vs_Wind_%2B_Solar_-_electricity_generation.svg/500px-2010-_Fossil_fuels_vs_Wind_%2B_Solar_-_electricity_generation.svg.png 2x" data-file-width="859" data-file-height="750" /></a><figcaption>In 2023, electricity generation from wind and solar sources was projected to exceed 30% by 2030.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-RMI&#95;20230613&#95;246-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-RMI_20230613-246"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>246<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></figcaption></figure>
<p>A December 2022 report by the IEA forecasts that over 2022-2027, renewables are seen growing by almost 2,400 GW in its main forecast, equal to the entire installed power capacity of China in 2021. This is an 85% acceleration from the previous five years, and almost 30% higher than what the IEA forecast in its 2021 report, making its largest ever upward revision. Renewables are set to account for over 90% of global electricity capacity expansion over the forecast period.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-IEA-3&#95;88-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-IEA-3-88"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>88<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> To achieve net zero emissions by 2050, IEA believes that 90% of global electricity generation will need to be produced from renewable sources.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:0&#95;19-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:0-19"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>19<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>In June 2022, IEA Executive Director <a href="/wiki/Fatih_Birol" title="Fatih Birol">Fatih Birol</a> said that countries should invest more in renewables to "ease the pressure on consumers from high fossil fuel prices, make our energy systems more secure, and get the world on track to reach our climate goals."<sup id="cite&#95;ref-247" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-247"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>247<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p><a href="/wiki/REPowerEU" title="REPowerEU">REPowerEU</a>, the EU plan to escape <a href="/wiki/Russia_in_the_European_energy_sector" title="Russia in the European energy sector">dependence on fossil Russian gas</a>, is expected to call for much more <a href="/wiki/Green_hydrogen" title="Green hydrogen">green hydrogen</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-248" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-248"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>248<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>After a transitional period,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-249" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-249"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>249<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> renewable energy production is expected to make up most of the world's energy production. In 2018, the risk management firm, <a href="/wiki/DNV_GL" class="mw-redirect" title="DNV GL">DNV GL</a>, forecasts that the world's primary <a href="/wiki/Energy_mix" title="Energy mix">energy mix</a> will be split equally between fossil and non-fossil sources by 2050.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-250" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-250"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>250<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Middle eastern nations are also planning on reducing their reliance fossil fuel. Many planned green projects will contribute in 26% of energy supply for the region by 2050 achieving emission reductions equal to 1.1 Gt CO<sub>2</sub>/year.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:14&#95;251-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:14-251"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>251<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Massive Renewable Energy Projects in the Middle East:<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:14&#95;251-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:14-251"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>251<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<ul><li>Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum Solar Park in Dubai, UAE</li>
<li>Shuaibah Two (2) Solar Facility in Mecca Province, Saudi Arabia</li>
<li>NEOM Green Hydrogen Project in NEOM, Saudi Arabia</li>
<li>Gulf of Suez Wind Power Project in Suez, Egypt</li>
<li>Al-Ajban Solar Park in Abu Dhabi, UAE</li></ul>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Demand">Demand</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=46" title="Edit section: Demand"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>In July 2014, the <a href="/wiki/World_Wide_Fund_for_Nature" title="World Wide Fund for Nature">WWF</a> and the <a href="/wiki/World_Resources_Institute" title="World Resources Institute">World Resources Institute</a> convened a discussion among a number of major US companies who had declared their intention to increase their use of renewable energy. These discussions identified a number of "principles" which companies seeking greater access to renewable energy considered important market deliverables. These principles included choice (between suppliers and between products), cost competitiveness, longer term fixed price supplies, access to third-party financing vehicles, and collaboration.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-252" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-252"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>252<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>UK statistics released in September 2020 noted that "the proportion of demand met from renewables varies from a low of 3.4 per cent (for transport, mainly from biofuels) to highs of over 20 per cent for 'other final users', which is largely the service and commercial sectors that consume relatively large quantities of electricity, and industry".<sup id="cite&#95;ref-253" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-253"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>253<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>In some locations, individual households can opt to purchase renewable energy through a <a href="/wiki/Consumer_green_energy_program" title="Consumer green energy program">consumer green energy program</a>.
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Developing_countries">Developing countries</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=47" title="Edit section: Developing countries"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<div class="excerpt-block"><style data-mw-deduplicate="TemplateStyles:r1066933788">.mw-parser-output .excerpt-hat .mw-editsection-like{font-style:normal}</style><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable dablink excerpt-hat selfref">This section is an excerpt from <a href="/wiki/Renewable_energy_in_developing_countries" title="Renewable energy in developing countries">Renewable energy in developing countries</a>.<span class="mw-editsection-like plainlinks"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a class="external text" href="https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy_in_developing_countries&amp;action=edit">edit</a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div><div class="excerpt">
<p><a href="/wiki/Renewable_energy_in_developing_countries" title="Renewable energy in developing countries">Renewable energy in developing countries</a> is an increasingly used alternative to <a href="/wiki/Fossil_fuel" title="Fossil fuel">fossil fuel</a> energy, as these countries scale up their energy supplies and address <a href="/wiki/Energy_poverty" title="Energy poverty">energy poverty</a>. Renewable energy technology was once seen as unaffordable for developing countries.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-254" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-254"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>254<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> However, since 2015, investment in non-hydro renewable energy has been higher in <a href="/wiki/Developing_countries" class="mw-redirect" title="Developing countries">developing countries</a> than in developed countries, and comprised 54% of global renewable energy investment in 2019.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-255" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-255"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>255<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The <a href="/wiki/International_Energy_Agency" title="International Energy Agency">International Energy Agency</a> forecasts that renewable energy will provide the majority of energy supply growth through 2030 in Africa and Central and South America, and 42% of supply growth in China.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-256" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-256"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>256<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Most developing countries have abundant renewable energy resources, including <a href="/wiki/Solar_energy" title="Solar energy">solar energy</a>, <a href="/wiki/Wind_power" title="Wind power">wind power</a>, <a href="/wiki/Geothermal_energy" title="Geothermal energy">geothermal energy</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Biomass" title="Biomass">biomass</a>, as well as the ability to manufacture the relatively labor-intensive systems that harness these. By developing such energy sources developing countries can reduce their dependence on oil and natural gas, creating energy portfolios that are less vulnerable to price rises. In many circumstances, these investments can be less expensive than fossil fuel energy systems.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Renewable&#95;energy&#95;in&#95;developing&#95;countries&#95;mdg&#95;257-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Renewable_energy_in_developing_countries_mdg-257"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>257<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
</div></div>
<p>In Kenya, the <a href="/wiki/Olkaria_V_Geothermal_Power_Station" title="Olkaria V Geothermal Power Station">Olkaria V Geothermal Power Station</a> is one of the largest in the world.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-258" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-258"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>258<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The <a href="/wiki/Grand_Ethiopian_Renaissance_Dam" title="Grand Ethiopian Renaissance Dam">Grand Ethiopia Renaissance Dam project</a> incorporates wind turbines.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-259" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-259"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>259<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Once completed, Morocco's <a href="/wiki/Ouarzazate_Solar_Power_Station" title="Ouarzazate Solar Power Station">Ouarzazate Solar Power Station</a> is projected to provide power to over a million people.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-260" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-260"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>260<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Policy">Policy</h2><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=48" title="Edit section: Policy"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:2021_Death_rates,_by_energy_source.svg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/2021_Death_rates%2C_by_energy_source.svg/500px-2021_Death_rates%2C_by_energy_source.svg.png" decoding="async" width="400" height="225" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/2021_Death_rates%2C_by_energy_source.svg/960px-2021_Death_rates%2C_by_energy_source.svg.png 2x" data-file-width="1200" data-file-height="675" /></a><figcaption>Deaths caused as a result of <a href="/wiki/Fossil_fuel" title="Fossil fuel">fossil fuel</a> use (areas of rectangles in chart) greatly exceed those resulting from production of renewable energy (rectangles barely visible in chart).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-OWID&#95;SafestEnergy&#95;2021&#95;261-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-OWID_SafestEnergy_2021-261"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>261<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></figcaption></figure>
<p>Policies to support renewable energy have been vital in their expansion. Where Europe dominated in establishing <a href="/wiki/Energy_policy" title="Energy policy">energy policy</a> in the early 2000s, most countries around the world now have some form of energy policy.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-IEA-2019&#95;262-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-IEA-2019-262"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>262<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The <a href="/wiki/International_Renewable_Energy_Agency" title="International Renewable Energy Agency">International Renewable Energy Agency</a> (IRENA) is an <a href="/wiki/Intergovernmental_organization" class="mw-redirect" title="Intergovernmental organization">intergovernmental organization</a> for promoting the adoption of renewable energy worldwide. It aims to provide concrete policy advice and facilitate <a href="/wiki/Capacity_building" title="Capacity building">capacity building</a> and technology transfer. IRENA was formed in 2009, with 75&#160;countries signing the charter of IRENA.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-263" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-263"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>263<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> As of April 2019, IRENA has 160&#160;member states.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-264" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-264"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>264<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The then United Nations Secretary-General <a href="/wiki/Ban_Ki-moon" title="Ban Ki-moon">Ban Ki-moon</a> has said that renewable energy can lift the poorest nations to new levels of prosperity.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-renewableenergyworld1&#95;265-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-renewableenergyworld1-265"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>265<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The 2015 <a href="/wiki/Paris_Agreement" title="Paris Agreement">Paris Agreement</a> on climate change motivated many countries to develop or improve renewable energy policies.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEREN21&#95;Renewables&#95;Global&#95;Futures&#95;Report2017&#95;266-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEREN21_Renewables_Global_Futures_Report2017-266"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>266<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In 2017, a total of 121 countries adopted some form of renewable energy policy.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-IEA-2019&#95;262-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-IEA-2019-262"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>262<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> National targets that year existed in 176 countries.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEREN21&#95;Renewables&#95;Global&#95;Futures&#95;Report2017&#95;266-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEREN21_Renewables_Global_Futures_Report2017-266"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>266<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In addition, there is also a wide range of policies at the state/provincial, and local levels.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-FOOTNOTEREN21&#95;Renewables&#95;Global&#95;Status&#95;Report201113–14&#95;131-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEREN21_Renewables_Global_Status_Report201113–14-131"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>131<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Some <a href="/wiki/Public_utility" title="Public utility">public utilities</a> help plan or install <a href="/wiki/Home_energy_upgrades_from_public_utilities" title="Home energy upgrades from public utilities">residential energy upgrades</a>.
</p><p>Many national, state and local governments have created <a href="/wiki/Green_bank_(financial_institution)" class="mw-redirect" title="Green bank (financial institution)">green banks</a>. A green bank is a quasi-public financial institution that uses public capital to leverage private investment in clean energy technologies.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-267" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-267"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>267<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Green banks use a variety of financial tools to bridge market gaps that hinder the deployment of clean energy.
</p><p>Global and national policies related to renewable energy can be divided based on sectors, such as agriculture, transport, buildings, industry:
</p><p><i>Climate neutrality</i> (<a href="/wiki/Net_zero_emissions" class="mw-redirect" title="Net zero emissions">net zero emissions</a>) by the year 2050 is the main goal of the <a href="/wiki/European_Green_Deal" title="European Green Deal">European Green Deal</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-268" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-268"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>268<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> For the European Union to reach their target of climate neutrality, one goal is to decarbonise its energy system by aiming to achieve "net-zero <a href="/wiki/Greenhouse_gas_emissions" title="Greenhouse gas emissions">greenhouse gas emissions</a> by 2050."<sup id="cite&#95;ref-269" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-269"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>269<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Finance">Finance</h2><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=49" title="Edit section: Finance"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1273380762/mw-parser-output/.tmulti" /><div class="thumb tmulti tright"><div class="thumbinner multiimageinner" style="width:392px;max-width:392px"><div class="trow"><div class="tsingle" style="width:196px;max-width:196px"><div class="thumbimage" style="height:106px;overflow:hidden"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:20210119_Renewable_energy_investment_-_2004-_BloombergNEF.svg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/20210119_Renewable_energy_investment_-_2004-_BloombergNEF.svg/250px-20210119_Renewable_energy_investment_-_2004-_BloombergNEF.svg.png" decoding="async" width="194" height="106" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/20210119_Renewable_energy_investment_-_2004-_BloombergNEF.svg/500px-20210119_Renewable_energy_investment_-_2004-_BloombergNEF.svg.png 2x" data-file-width="1230" data-file-height="675" /></a></span></div><div class="thumbcaption"><a href="/wiki/Electrification" title="Electrification">Electrified</a> transport and renewable energy are key areas of investment for climate change mitigation via an <a href="/wiki/Energy_transition" title="Energy transition">energy transition</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-BloombergNEF&#95;20230126&#95;230-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-BloombergNEF_20230126-230"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>230<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-BNEF&#95;20240130&#95;231-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-BNEF_20240130-231"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>231<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-BloombergNEF&#95;20250131&#95;232-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-BloombergNEF_20250131-232"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>232<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></div></div><div class="tsingle" style="width:192px;max-width:192px"><div class="thumbimage" style="height:106px;overflow:hidden"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:2000-_Clean_energy_patent_filings_-_by_country.svg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/2000-_Clean_energy_patent_filings_-_by_country.svg/250px-2000-_Clean_energy_patent_filings_-_by_country.svg.png" decoding="async" width="190" height="107" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/2000-_Clean_energy_patent_filings_-_by_country.svg/500px-2000-_Clean_energy_patent_filings_-_by_country.svg.png 2x" data-file-width="1200" data-file-height="675" /></a></span></div><div class="thumbcaption">China's count of multi-country patent filings has surged since the mid-2010s, leading in batteries and solar, though Europe still dominates in wind energy and smart grids.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-NYTimes&#95;20250814&#95;270-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NYTimes_20250814-270"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>270<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> China also leads in highly cited publications in global peer-reviewed journals.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-NYTimes&#95;20250814&#95;270-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NYTimes_20250814-270"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>270<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></div></div></div></div></div>
<p>The <a href="/wiki/International_Renewable_Energy_Agency" title="International Renewable Energy Agency">International Renewable Energy Agency</a>'s (IRENA) 2023 report on renewable energy finance highlights steady investment growth since 2018: USD 348 billion in 2020 (a 5.6% increase from 2019), USD 430 billion in 2021 (24% up from 2020), and USD 499 billion in 2022 (16% higher). This trend is driven by increasing recognition of renewable energy's role in mitigating climate change and enhancing <a href="/wiki/Energy_security" title="Energy security">energy security</a>, along with investor interest in alternatives to fossil fuels. Policies such as <a href="/wiki/Feed-in_tariff" title="Feed-in tariff">feed-in tariffs</a> in China and Vietnam have significantly increased renewable adoption. Furthermore, from 2013 to 2022, installation costs for solar photovoltaic (PV), onshore wind, and offshore wind dropped by 69%, 33%, and 45%, respectively, making renewables more cost-effective.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-271" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-271"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>271<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-:1&#95;67-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:1-67"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>67<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Between 2013 and 2022, the renewable energy sector underwent a significant realignment of investment priorities. Investment in solar and wind energy technologies markedly increased. In contrast, other renewable technologies such as hydropower (including <a href="/wiki/Pumped-storage_hydropower" class="mw-redirect" title="Pumped-storage hydropower">pumped storage hydropower</a>), <a href="/wiki/Biomass_(energy)" title="Biomass (energy)">biomass</a>, <a href="/wiki/Biofuel" title="Biofuel">biofuels</a>, <a href="/wiki/Geothermal_energy" title="Geothermal energy">geothermal</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Marine_energy" title="Marine energy">marine energy</a> experienced a substantial decrease in financial investment. Notably, from 2017 to 2022, investment in these alternative renewable technologies declined by 45%, falling from USD 35 billion to USD 17 billion.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:1&#95;67-3" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:1-67"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>67<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>In 2023, the renewable energy sector experienced a significant surge in investments, particularly in solar and wind technologies, totaling approximately USD 200 billion—a 75% increase from the previous year. The increased investments in 2023 contributed between 1% and 4% to the GDP in key regions including the United States, China, the European Union, and India.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-272" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-272"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>272<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The energy sector receives investments of approximately USD 3 trillion each year, with USD 1.9 trillion directed towards <a href="/wiki/Sustainable_energy" title="Sustainable energy">clean energy</a> technologies and infrastructure. To meet the targets set in the <a href="/wiki/Net_zero_emissions" class="mw-redirect" title="Net zero emissions">Net Zero Emissions</a> (NZE) Scenario by 2035, this investment must increase to USD 5.3 trillion per year.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-273" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-273"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>273<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page: 15">&#58;&#8202;15&#8202;</span></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Debates">Debates</h2><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=50" title="Edit section: Debates"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main articles: <a href="/wiki/Renewable_energy_debate" title="Renewable energy debate">Renewable energy debate</a>, <a href="/wiki/Green_job" title="Green job">Green job</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Intermittent_power_source" class="mw-redirect" title="Intermittent power source">Intermittent power source</a></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Further information: <a href="/wiki/Climate_change_mitigation#Overviews,_strategies_and_comparisons_of_measures" title="Climate change mitigation">Climate change mitigation §&#160;Overviews, strategies and comparisons of measures</a></div>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Nuclear_power_proposed_as_renewable_energy">Nuclear power proposed as renewable energy</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=51" title="Edit section: Nuclear power proposed as renewable energy"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:2011-05-10_18-57-46_Switzerland_-_Wil_crop.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/2011-05-10_18-57-46_Switzerland_-_Wil_crop.jpg/250px-2011-05-10_18-57-46_Switzerland_-_Wil_crop.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="250" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/2011-05-10_18-57-46_Switzerland_-_Wil_crop.jpg/500px-2011-05-10_18-57-46_Switzerland_-_Wil_crop.jpg 2x" data-file-width="1600" data-file-height="1600" /></a><figcaption>The <a href="/wiki/Leibstadt_Nuclear_Power_Plant" title="Leibstadt Nuclear Power Plant">Leibstadt Nuclear Power Plant</a> in Switzerland</figcaption></figure>
<div class="excerpt-block"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1066933788" /><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable dablink excerpt-hat selfref">This section is an excerpt from <a href="/wiki/Nuclear_power_proposed_as_renewable_energy" title="Nuclear power proposed as renewable energy">Nuclear power proposed as renewable energy</a>.<span class="mw-editsection-like plainlinks"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a class="external text" href="https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Nuclear_power_proposed_as_renewable_energy&amp;action=edit">edit</a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div><div class="excerpt">
<p>Whether <a href="/wiki/Nuclear_power" title="Nuclear power">nuclear power</a> should be considered a form of renewable energy is an ongoing subject of debate. Legal definitions of renewable energy usually exclude nuclear energy technologies, with the notable exception of the U.S. state of <a href="/wiki/Utah" title="Utah">Utah</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Nuclear&#95;power&#95;proposed&#95;as&#95;renewable&#95;energy&#95;Utah&#95;House&#95;Bill&#95;430,&#95;Session&#95;198&#95;274-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Nuclear_power_proposed_as_renewable_energy_Utah_House_Bill_430,_Session_198-274"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>274<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Dictionary-sourced definitions of renewable <a href="/wiki/Energy_technologies" class="mw-redirect" title="Energy technologies">energy technologies</a> often omit or explicitly exclude mention of nuclear energy sources, with an exception made for the natural nuclear <a href="/wiki/Decay_heat" title="Decay heat">decay heat</a> generated <a href="/wiki/Geothermal_energy" title="Geothermal energy">within the Earth</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-275" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-275"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>275<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-Nuclear&#95;power&#95;proposed&#95;as&#95;renewable&#95;energy&#95;renewalt&#95;basics&#95;276-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Nuclear_power_proposed_as_renewable_energy_renewalt_basics-276"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>276<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The most common fuel used in conventional <a href="/wiki/Nuclear_power_station" class="mw-redirect" title="Nuclear power station">nuclear fission power stations</a>, <a href="/wiki/Uranium-235" title="Uranium-235">uranium-235</a> is "non-renewable" according to the United States' <a href="/wiki/Energy_Information_Administration" title="Energy Information Administration">Energy Information Administration</a>, the organization, however, is silent on the recycled <a href="/wiki/MOX_fuel" title="MOX fuel">MOX fuel</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Nuclear&#95;power&#95;proposed&#95;as&#95;renewable&#95;energy&#95;renewalt&#95;basics&#95;276-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Nuclear_power_proposed_as_renewable_energy_renewalt_basics-276"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>276<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The <a href="/wiki/National_Renewable_Energy_Laboratory" class="mw-redirect" title="National Renewable Energy Laboratory">National Renewable Energy Laboratory</a> does not mention nuclear power in its "energy basics" definition.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-277" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-277"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>277<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>In 1987, the <a href="/wiki/Brundtland_Commission" title="Brundtland Commission">Brundtland Commission</a> (WCED) classified fission reactors that produce more <a href="/wiki/Fissile" class="mw-redirect" title="Fissile">fissile</a> <a href="/wiki/Nuclear_fuel" title="Nuclear fuel">nuclear fuel</a> than they consume (<a href="/wiki/Breeder_reactor" title="Breeder reactor">breeder reactors</a>, and if developed, <a href="/wiki/Fusion_power" title="Fusion power">fusion power</a>) among conventional <a href="/wiki/Renewable_energy_sources" class="mw-redirect" title="Renewable energy sources">renewable energy sources</a>, such as <a href="/wiki/Solar_power" title="Solar power">solar power</a> and <a href="/wiki/Hydropower" title="Hydropower">hydropower</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Nuclear&#95;power&#95;proposed&#95;as&#95;renewable&#95;energy&#95;Brundtland&#95;278-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Nuclear_power_proposed_as_renewable_energy_Brundtland-278"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>278<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The monitoring and storage of <a href="/wiki/Radioactive" class="mw-redirect" title="Radioactive">radioactive</a> waste products is also required upon the use of other renewable energy sources, such as geothermal energy.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-279" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-279"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>279<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
</div></div>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Geopolitics">Geopolitics</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=52" title="Edit section: Geopolitics"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">See also: <a href="/wiki/Electrostate" title="Electrostate">Electrostate</a></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Further information: <a href="/wiki/Russia_in_the_European_energy_sector" title="Russia in the European energy sector">Russia in the European energy sector</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:TREC-Map-en.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/TREC-Map-en.jpg/250px-TREC-Map-en.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="197" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/TREC-Map-en.jpg/500px-TREC-Map-en.jpg 2x" data-file-width="2000" data-file-height="1575" /></a><figcaption>A concept of a <a href="/wiki/Super_grid" title="Super grid">super grid</a></figcaption></figure>
<p>The <a href="/wiki/Geopolitics" title="Geopolitics">geopolitical</a> impact of the growing use of renewable energy is a subject of ongoing debate and research.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-280" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-280"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>280<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Many countries wealthy in oil, such as <a href="/wiki/Qatar" title="Qatar">Qatar</a>, <a href="/wiki/Russia" title="Russia">Russia</a>, <a href="/wiki/Saudi_Arabia" title="Saudi Arabia">Saudi Arabia</a> and <a href="/wiki/Norway" title="Norway">Norway</a>, and are able to exert diplomatic or geopolitical influence as a result of their oil. Most of these countries are expected to be among the geopolitical "losers" of the energy transition, although some, like Norway, are also significant producers and exporters of renewable energy. Fossil fuels and the infrastructure to extract them may, in the long term, become <a href="/wiki/Stranded_asset" title="Stranded asset">stranded assets</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-281" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-281"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>281<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> It has been speculated that countries dependent on fossil fuel revenue may one day find it in their interests to quickly <a href="/wiki/Fire_sale" title="Fire sale">sell off</a> their remaining fossil fuels.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-282" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-282"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>282<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Conversely, nations abundant in renewable resources, and the minerals required for renewables technology, are expected to gain influence.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-283" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-283"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>283<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-284" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-284"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>284<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In particular, <a href="/wiki/China" title="China">China</a> has become the world's dominant manufacturer of the technology needed to produce or store renewable energy, especially <a href="/wiki/Solar_panel" title="Solar panel">solar panels</a>, <a href="/wiki/Wind_turbine" title="Wind turbine">wind turbines</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Lithium-ion_battery" title="Lithium-ion battery">lithium-ion batteries</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-285" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-285"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>285<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Nations rich in solar and wind energy could become major energy exporters.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-cb1&#95;286-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-cb1-286"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>286<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Some may produce and export <a href="/wiki/Green_hydrogen" title="Green hydrogen">green hydrogen</a>,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-287" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-287"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>287<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-cb1&#95;286-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-cb1-286"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>286<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> although electricity is projected to be the dominant <a href="/wiki/Energy_carrier" title="Energy carrier">energy carrier</a> in 2050, accounting for almost 50% of total energy consumption (up from 22% in 2015).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-288" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-288"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>288<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Countries with large uninhabited areas such as Australia, China, and many African and Middle Eastern countries have a potential for huge installations of renewable energy. The production of renewable energy technologies requires <a href="/wiki/Rare-earth_element" title="Rare-earth element">rare-earth elements</a> with new supply chains.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-289" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-289"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>289<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Countries in Africa with already weak governments that rely on fossil fuel revenue may face even higher political instability or popular unrest. Analysts consider <a href="/wiki/Nigeria" title="Nigeria">Nigeria</a>, <a href="/wiki/Angola" title="Angola">Angola</a>, <a href="/wiki/Chad" title="Chad">Chad</a>, <a href="/wiki/Gabon" title="Gabon">Gabon</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Sudan" title="Sudan">Sudan</a>, all countries with a history of <a href="/wiki/Coup_d%27%C3%A9tat" title="Coup d&#39;état">military coups</a>, to be at risk of instability due to dwindling oil income.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-290" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-290"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>290<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>A study found that transition from fossil fuels to renewable energy systems reduces risks from mining, trade and political dependence because renewable energy systems don't need fuel – they depend on trade only for the acquisition of materials and components during construction.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-291" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-291"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>291<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>In October 2021, European Commissioner for Climate Action <a href="/wiki/Frans_Timmermans" title="Frans Timmermans">Frans Timmermans</a> suggested "the best answer" to the <a href="/wiki/2021_global_energy_crisis" class="mw-redirect" title="2021 global energy crisis">2021 global energy crisis</a> is "to reduce our reliance on fossil fuels."<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Timmermans&#95;292-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Timmermans-292"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>292<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> He said those blaming the <a href="/wiki/European_Green_Deal" title="European Green Deal">European Green Deal</a> were doing so "for perhaps ideological reasons or sometimes economic reasons in protecting their vested interests."<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Timmermans&#95;292-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Timmermans-292"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>292<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Some critics blamed the <a href="/wiki/European_Union_Emissions_Trading_System" title="European Union Emissions Trading System">European Union Emissions Trading System</a> (EU ETS) and <a href="/wiki/Nuclear_power_phase-out" title="Nuclear power phase-out">closure of nuclear plants</a> for contributing to the energy crisis.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-293" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-293"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>293<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-294" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-294"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>294<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-295" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-295"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>295<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> European Commission President <a href="/wiki/Ursula_von_der_Leyen" title="Ursula von der Leyen">Ursula von der Leyen</a> said that Europe is "too reliant" on <a href="/wiki/Natural_gas" title="Natural gas">natural gas</a> and too <a href="/wiki/List_of_countries_by_natural_gas_imports" title="List of countries by natural gas imports">dependent on natural gas imports</a>. According to Von der Leyen, "The answer has to do with diversifying our suppliers ... and, crucially, with speeding up the transition to clean energy."<sup id="cite&#95;ref-296" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-296"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>296<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Energy transition can also improve a country's <a href="/wiki/Energy_security" title="Energy security">energy security</a> and <a href="/wiki/Energy_independence" title="Energy independence">energy independence</a>, mitigating impacts from fossil fuel markets or geopolitical pressure from oil producers.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-297" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-297"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>297<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-298" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-298"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>298<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The proliferation of renewable energy could also weaken petrostates' trading power and their global influence. As the world becomes more electrified, renewable technologies will be embedded into the national economies of various markets, especially developing countries, removing the leverage petrostates have on the global energy structure.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-299" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-299"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>299<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-300" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-300"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>300<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-301" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-301"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>301<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Metal_and_mineral_extraction">Metal and mineral extraction</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=53" title="Edit section: Metal and mineral extraction"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">See also: <a href="/wiki/Environmental_footprint_of_electric_cars" class="mw-redirect" title="Environmental footprint of electric cars">Environmental footprint of electric cars</a> and <a href="/wiki/Rare-earth_element#Environmental_considerations" title="Rare-earth element">Rare-earth element §&#160;Environmental considerations</a></div>
<p>The <a href="/wiki/Energy_transition" title="Energy transition">transition</a> to renewable energy requires increased extraction of certain metals and minerals. Like all mining, this impacts the environment<sup id="cite&#95;ref-302" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-302"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>302<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and can lead to <a href="/wiki/Environmental_conflict" title="Environmental conflict">environmental conflict</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-303" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-303"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>303<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> For example, lithium mining uses around 65% of the water in the <a href="/wiki/Salar_de_Atacama" title="Salar de Atacama">Salar de Atacama</a> desert forcing farmers and <a href="/wiki/Llama" title="Llama">llama</a> herders to abandon their ancestral settlements and creating environment degradation,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-304" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-304"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>304<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> in several African countries, the green energy transition has created a mining boom, causing deforestation, and threatening already endangered species.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-305" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-305"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>305<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>  Wind power requires large amounts of <a href="/wiki/Copper" title="Copper">copper</a> and <a href="/wiki/Zinc" title="Zinc">zinc</a>, as well as smaller amounts of the rarer metal <a href="/wiki/Neodymium" title="Neodymium">neodymium</a>. Solar power is less resource-intensive, but still requires significant amounts of aluminum. The expansion of electrical grids requires both copper and aluminum. Batteries, which are critical to enable storage of renewable energy, use large quantities of copper, <a href="/wiki/Nickel" title="Nickel">nickel</a>, aluminum and <a href="/wiki/Graphite" title="Graphite">graphite</a>. Demand for lithium is expected to grow 42-fold from 2020 to 2040. Demand for nickel, <a href="/wiki/Cobalt" title="Cobalt">cobalt</a> and graphite is expected to grow by a factor of about 20–25.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-IEA&#95;minerals&#95;306-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-IEA_minerals-306"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>306<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> For each of the most relevant minerals and metals, its mining is dominated by a single country: <a href="/wiki/Copper_mining_in_Chile" title="Copper mining in Chile">copper in Chile</a>, <a href="/wiki/Nickel_mining_in_Indonesia" title="Nickel mining in Indonesia">nickel in Indonesia</a>, <a href="/wiki/Rare_earth_industry_in_China" class="mw-redirect" title="Rare earth industry in China">rare earths in China</a>, <a href="/wiki/Mining_in_the_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo" class="mw-redirect" title="Mining in the Democratic Republic of the Congo">cobalt in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC)</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Lithium_mining_in_Australia" title="Lithium mining in Australia">lithium in Australia</a>. China dominates <a href="/wiki/Mineral_processing" title="Mineral processing">processing</a> of all of these.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-IEA&#95;minerals&#95;306-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-IEA_minerals-306"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>306<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Recycling these metals after the devices they are embedded in are spent is essential to create a <a href="/wiki/Circular_economy" title="Circular economy">circular economy</a> and ensure renewable energy is sustainable. By 2040, recycled copper, lithium, cobalt, and nickel from spent batteries could reduce combined primary supply requirements for these minerals by around 10%.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-IEA&#95;minerals&#95;306-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-IEA_minerals-306"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>306<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>A controversial approach is <a href="/wiki/Deep_sea_mining" title="Deep sea mining">deep sea mining</a>. Minerals can be collected from new sources like <a href="/wiki/Polymetallic_nodules" class="mw-redirect" title="Polymetallic nodules">polymetallic nodules</a> lying on the <a href="/wiki/Seabed" title="Seabed">seabed</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-307" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-307"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>307<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> This would damage local biodiversity,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-308" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-308"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>308<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> but proponents point out that biomass on resource-rich seabeds is much scarcer than in the mining regions on land, which are often found in vulnerable habitats like rainforests.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-309" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-309"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>309<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Due to co-occurrence of rare-earth and radioactive elements (<a href="/wiki/Thorium" title="Thorium">thorium</a>, <a href="/wiki/Uranium" title="Uranium">uranium</a> and <a href="/wiki/Radium" title="Radium">radium</a>), rare-earth mining results in production of low-level <a href="/wiki/Radioactive_waste" title="Radioactive waste">radioactive waste</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-310" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-310"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>310<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Conservation_areas">Conservation areas</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=54" title="Edit section: Conservation areas"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>Installations used to produce wind, solar and hydropower are an increasing threat to key conservation areas, with facilities built in areas set aside for nature conservation and other environmentally sensitive areas. They are often much larger than fossil fuel power plants, needing areas of land up to 10 times greater than coal or gas to produce equivalent energy amounts.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-311" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-311"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>311<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> More than 2000 renewable energy facilities are built, and more are under construction, in areas of environmental importance and threaten the habitats of plant and animal species across the globe. The authors' team emphasized that their work should not be interpreted as anti-renewables because renewable energy is crucial for reducing carbon emissions. The key is ensuring that renewable energy facilities are built in places where they do not damage biodiversity.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-312" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-312"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>312<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>In 2020 scientists published a <a href="/wiki/World_map" title="World map">world map</a> of areas that contain renewable energy materials as well as estimations of their overlaps with "Key Biodiversity Areas", "Remaining Wilderness" and "<a href="/wiki/Protected_area" title="Protected area">Protected Areas</a>". The authors assessed that careful <a href="/wiki/Strategic_planning" title="Strategic planning">strategic planning</a> is needed.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-313" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-313"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>313<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-314" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-314"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>314<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-315" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-315"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>315<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Impact_of_climate_change_on_renewable_energy_production">Impact of climate change on renewable energy production</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=55" title="Edit section: Impact of climate change on renewable energy production"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>Climate change is making weather patterns less predictable. This can seriously hamper the use of renewable energy. For example, in the year 2023, in Sudan and Namibia, hydropower production dropped by more than half due to drastic reduction in rainfall, in China, India and some regions in Africa unusual weather phenomena reduced the amount of produced wind energy, heatwaves and clouds reduce the effectiveness of solar panels, melting glaciers create problems to hydropower. Nuclear energy is also affected as drought creates water shortage, so nuclear power plants sometimes do not have enough water for cooling.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-316" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-316"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>316<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Society_and_culture">Society and culture</h2><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=56" title="Edit section: Society and culture"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Public_support">Public support</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=57" title="Edit section: Public support"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1273380762/mw-parser-output/.tmulti" /><div class="thumb tmulti tright"><div class="thumbinner multiimageinner" style="width:392px;max-width:392px"><div class="trow"><div class="tsingle" style="width:196px;max-width:196px"><div class="thumbimage" style="height:251px;overflow:hidden"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:Most_people_say_countries_should_back_renewable_energy_to_fight_climate_change.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Most_people_say_countries_should_back_renewable_energy_to_fight_climate_change.jpg/250px-Most_people_say_countries_should_back_renewable_energy_to_fight_climate_change.jpg" decoding="async" width="194" height="251" class="mw-file-element" 2x" data-file-width="1700" data-file-height="2200" /></a></span></div><div class="thumbcaption">Most respondents to a climate survey conducted in 2021-2022 by the <a href="/wiki/European_Investment_Bank" title="European Investment Bank">European Investment Bank</a> say countries should back renewable energy to fight climate change.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-317" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-317"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>317<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></div></div><div class="tsingle" style="width:192px;max-width:192px"><div class="thumbimage" style="height:251px;overflow:hidden"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:Renewable_energies_are_considered_an_investment_priority_in_the_European_Union,_China_and,_to_a_lesser_extent,_the_United_States.png" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/Renewable_energies_are_considered_an_investment_priority_in_the_European_Union%2C_China_and%2C_to_a_lesser_extent%2C_the_United_States.png/250px-Renewable_energies_are_considered_an_investment_priority_in_the_European_Union%2C_China_and%2C_to_a_lesser_extent%2C_the_United_States.png"
