2x" data-file-width="1566" data-file-height="2080" /></a></span></div><div class="thumbcaption">The same survey a year later shows that renewable energy is considered an investment priority in the European Union, China and the United States.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-318" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-318"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>318<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></div></div></div></div></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:202307_Survey_-_comfortable_with_solar_wind_nuclear_in_my_community.svg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/202307_Survey_-_comfortable_with_solar_wind_nuclear_in_my_community.svg/250px-202307_Survey_-_comfortable_with_solar_wind_nuclear_in_my_community.svg.png" decoding="async" width="250" height="141" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/202307_Survey_-_comfortable_with_solar_wind_nuclear_in_my_community.svg/500px-202307_Survey_-_comfortable_with_solar_wind_nuclear_in_my_community.svg.png 2x" data-file-width="1200" data-file-height="675" /></a><figcaption>Acceptance of wind and solar facilities in one's community is stronger among U.S. Democrats (blue), while acceptance of nuclear power plants is stronger among U.S. Republicans (red).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-WashPost&#95;20231003&#95;319-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-WashPost_20231003-319"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>319<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></figcaption></figure>
<p>Solar power plants may compete with <a href="/wiki/Arable_land" title="Arable land">arable land</a>,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-van&#95;Zalk&#95;Behrens&#95;320-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-van_Zalk_Behrens-320"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>320<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-321" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-321"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>321<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> while on-shore <a href="/wiki/Wind_farm" title="Wind farm">wind farms</a> often face opposition due to aesthetic concerns and noise.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-322" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-322"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>322<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-323" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-323"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>323<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Such opponents are often described as <a href="/wiki/NIMBY" title="NIMBY">NIMBYs</a> ("not in my back yard").<sup id="cite&#95;ref-324" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-324"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>324<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Some environmentalists are concerned about fatal collisions of birds and bats with wind turbines.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-325" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-325"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>325<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Although protests against new wind farms occasionally occur around the world, regional and national surveys generally find broad support for both solar and wind power.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-326" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-326"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>326<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-327" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-327"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>327<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-328" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-328"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>328<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p><a href="/wiki/Community_wind_energy" title="Community wind energy">Community-owned wind energy</a> is sometimes proposed as a way to increase local support for wind farms.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:6&#95;329-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:6-329"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>329<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> A 2011 UK Government document stated that "projects are generally more likely to succeed if they have broad public support and the consent of local communities. This means giving communities both a say and a stake."<sup id="cite&#95;ref-330" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-330"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>330<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In the 2000s and early 2010s, many renewable projects in Germany, Sweden and Denmark were owned by local communities, particularly through <a href="/wiki/Cooperative" title="Cooperative">cooperative</a> structures.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-331" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-331"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>331<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-332" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-332"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>332<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In the years since, more installations in Germany have been undertaken by large companies,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:6&#95;329-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:6-329"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>329<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> but community ownership remains strong in Denmark.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-333" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-333"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>333<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="History">History</h2><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=58" title="Edit section: History"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">See also: <a href="/wiki/History_of_climate_change_policy_and_politics" title="History of climate change policy and politics">History of climate change policy and politics</a></div>
<p>Prior to the development of coal in the mid 19th century, nearly all energy used was renewable. The oldest known use of renewable energy, in the form of traditional <a href="/wiki/Biomass_(energy)" title="Biomass (energy)">biomass</a> to <a href="/wiki/Control_of_fire_by_early_humans" title="Control of fire by early humans">fuel fires</a>, dates from more than a million years ago. The use of biomass for fire did not become commonplace until many hundreds of thousands of years later.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Fire&#95;334-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Fire-334"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>334<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The second oldest usage of renewable energy was probably harnessing the wind in order to drive ships over water. This practice can be traced back some 7000 years, to ships in the <a href="/wiki/Persian_Gulf" title="Persian Gulf">Persian Gulf</a> and on the <a href="/wiki/Nile" title="Nile">Nile</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-335" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-335"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>335<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Geothermal energy from <a href="/wiki/Hot_spring" title="Hot spring">hot springs</a> has been used for bathing since <a href="/wiki/Paleolithic" title="Paleolithic">Paleolithic</a> times and space heating since ancient Roman times.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-336" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-336"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>336<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Moving into the time of recorded history, the primary sources of traditional renewable energy were <a href="/wiki/Manual_labour" title="Manual labour">human labor</a>, <a href="/wiki/Animal_power" class="mw-redirect" title="Animal power">animal power</a>, <a href="/wiki/Water_power" class="mw-redirect" title="Water power">water power</a>, wind, in grain crushing <a href="/wiki/Windmill" title="Windmill">windmills</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Firewood" title="Firewood">firewood</a>, a traditional biomass.
</p><p>In 1885, <a href="/wiki/Werner_Siemens" class="mw-redirect" title="Werner Siemens">Werner Siemens</a>, commenting on the discovery of the <a href="/wiki/Photovoltaic_effect" title="Photovoltaic effect">photovoltaic effect</a> in the solid state, wrote:
</p>
<style data-mw-deduplicate="TemplateStyles:r1244412712">.mw-parser-output .templatequote{overflow:hidden;margin:1em 0;padding:0 32px}.mw-parser-output .templatequotecite{line-height:1.5em;text-align:left;margin-top:0}@media(min-width:500px){.mw-parser-output .templatequotecite{padding-left:1.6em}}</style><blockquote class="templatequote"><p>In conclusion, I would say that however great the scientific importance of this discovery may be, its practical value will be no less obvious when we reflect that the supply of solar energy is both without limit and without cost, and that it will continue to pour down upon us for countless ages after all the coal deposits of the earth have been exhausted and forgotten.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-337" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-337"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>337<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></p></blockquote>
<p><a href="/wiki/Max_Weber" title="Max Weber">Max Weber</a> mentioned the end of fossil fuel in the concluding paragraphs of his <a href="/wiki/Die_protestantische_Ethik_und_der_Geist_des_Kapitalismus" class="mw-redirect" title="Die protestantische Ethik und der Geist des Kapitalismus">Die protestantische Ethik und der Geist des Kapitalismus</a> (The Protestant Ethic and the Spirit of Capitalism), published in 1905.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-338" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-338"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>338<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Development of solar engines continued until the outbreak of World War I. The importance of solar energy was recognized in a 1911 <i><a href="/wiki/Scientific_American" title="Scientific American">Scientific American</a></i> article: "in the far distant future, <a href="/wiki/Fossil_fuel" title="Fossil fuel">natural fuels</a> having been exhausted [solar power] will remain as the only means of existence of the human race".<sup id="cite&#95;ref-339" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-339"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>339<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The theory of <a href="/wiki/Peak_oil" title="Peak oil">peak oil</a> was published in 1956.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-340" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-340"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>340<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In the 1970s environmentalists promoted the development of renewable energy both as a replacement for the eventual <a href="/wiki/Depletion_of_oil" class="mw-redirect" title="Depletion of oil">depletion of oil</a>, as well as for an escape from dependence on oil, and the first electricity-generating <a href="/wiki/Wind_turbine" title="Wind turbine">wind turbines</a> appeared. Solar had long been used for heating and cooling, but solar panels were too costly to build solar farms until 1980.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-341" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-341"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>341<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>New government spending, regulation and policies helped the renewables industry weather the <a href="/wiki/2008_financial_crisis" title="2008 financial crisis">2008 financial crisis</a> and the <a href="/wiki/Great_Recession" title="Great Recession">Great Recession</a> better than many other sectors.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-obama&#95;342-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-obama-342"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>342<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In 2022, renewables accounted for 30% of global electricity generation, up from 21% in 1985.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:3&#95;343-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:3-343"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>343<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Ancient_Historical_Examples">Ancient Historical Examples</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Renewable_energy&amp;action=edit&amp;section=59" title="Edit section: Ancient Historical Examples"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>Among the most notable historical uses of renewable energy (in the form of ancient and traditional methods), the following examples can be highlighted:
</p>
<ol><li>Windmills in <a href="/wiki/Europe" title="Europe">Europe</a> and <a href="/wiki/Asia" title="Asia">Asia</a> (such as the <a href="/wiki/Windmill" title="Windmill">windmills</a> of the <a href="/wiki/Netherlands" title="Netherlands">Netherlands</a> and <a href="/wiki/Nashtifan" title="Nashtifan">Nashtifan</a> in Iran).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-344" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-344"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>344<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The earliest discovered verified designs of windmills date back to Iran, between 700 and 900 CE.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-345" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-345"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>345<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-346" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-346"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>346<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-347" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-347"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>347<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></li>
<li>Water mills (<a href="/wiki/History_of_China" title="History of China">Ancient China</a> and <a href="/wiki/History_of_Iran" title="History of Iran">Ancient Persia</a>).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-348" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-348"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>348<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></li>
<li><a href="/wiki/Archimedes" title="Archimedes">Archimedes</a>' <a href="/wiki/Burning_glass" title="Burning glass">burning lens</a>.</li>
<li>Traditional cooling and ventilation systems based on <a href="/wiki/Windcatcher" title="Windcatcher">windcatchers</a> and <a href="/wiki/Solar_updraft_tower" title="Solar updraft tower">Solar updraft tower</a> (or <a href="/wiki/Solar_chimney" title="Solar chimney">Solar chimney</a>).</li>
<li>Traditional architecture aware of natural <a href="/wiki/Heat_transfer" title="Heat transfer">heat transfer</a> and natural <a href="/wiki/Energy_transformation" title="Energy transformation">energy transformation</a> processes.</li>
<li>Gravity-based <a href="/wiki/Fountain" title="Fountain">fountains</a>.</li>
<li>Using animal <a href="/wiki/Biomass_(energy)" title="Biomass (energy)">biomass</a> in ancient fuel bricks.</li>
<li>Solar ovens and furnaces in ancient China, India, Egypt, and Persia.</li>
<li>Solar energy applications for traditional agricultural processing (drying), engineering material properties (solar curing of <a href="/wiki/Pottery" title="Pottery">pottery</a> and <a href="/wiki/Ceramic" title="Ceramic">ceramics</a>), and ancient health practices (natural disinfection by <a href="/wiki/Sunlight" title="Sunlight">solar radiation</a>).</li>
<li>Long-distance gravitational water flow control in ancient <a href="/wiki/Qanat" title="Qanat">qanat technology</a> for water transport and supply.</li>
<li>Cargo and passenger transportation using <a href="/wiki/Sail" title="Sail">sails</a> on rivers, seas, and oceans.</li>
<li>Cargo and passenger transportation based on understanding water currents in rivers, seas, and oceans.</li>
<li>Using renewable vegetation (such as desert shrubs, agricultural waste, and pruned branches) for producing <a href="/wiki/Light" title="Light">light</a> and <a href="/wiki/Heat" title="Heat">heat</a>.</li>
<li>Using renewable oils (vegetable or animal-based) for producing light and heat.</li>
<li>Maximizing use of natural sunlight during the day and moonlight at night in building architecture for purposes such as lighting, decorative applications (e.g., reflective tilework, mirror work, and surface polishing on stone or brick), timekeeping (<a href="/wiki/Sundial" title="Sundial">sundials</a>, noon markers, <a href="/wiki/Prayer_times" class="mw-redirect" title="Prayer times">prayer time</a> indicators, seasonal change markers), etc.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-349" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-349"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>349<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></li></ol>

<li id="cite&#95;note-LazardPopSci&#95;costs-236"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-LazardPopSci_costs_236-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFChrobak2021" class="citation news cs1">Chrobak, Ula (28 January 2021). <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://www.popsci.com/story/environment/cheap-renewable-energy-vs-fossil-fuels/">"Solar power got cheap. So why aren't we using it more?"</a>. <i>Popular Science</i>. Infographics by Sara Chodosh. <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://web.archive.org/web/20210129144621/https://www.popsci.com/story/environment/cheap-renewable-energy-vs-fossil-fuels/">Archived</a> from the original on 29 January 2021.</cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Ajournal&amp;rft.genre=article&amp;rft.jtitle=Popular+Science&amp;rft.atitle=Solar+power+got+cheap.+So+why+aren%27t+we+using+it+more%3F&amp;rft.date=2021-01-28&amp;rft.aulast=Chrobak&amp;rft.aufirst=Ula&amp;rft&#95;id=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.popsci.com%2Fstory%2Fenvironment%2Fcheap-renewable-energy-vs-fossil-fuels%2F&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3ARenewable+energy" class="Z3988"></span> Chodosh's graphic is derived from data in <link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite class="citation web cs1"><a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://www.lazard.com/media/451419/lazards-levelized-cost-of-energy-version-140.pdf">"Lazard's Levelized Cost of Energy Version 14.0"</a> <span class="cs1-format">(PDF)</span>. <i>Lazard.com</i>. <li id="cite&#95;note-Nuclear&#95;power&#95;proposed&#95;as&#95;renewable&#95;energy&#95;Brundtland-278"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-Nuclear_power_proposed_as_renewable_energy_Brundtland_278-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFBrundtland1987" class="citation web cs1">Brundtland, Gro Harlem (20 March 1987). <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="http://www.un-documents.net/ocf-07.htm">"Chapter 7: Energy: Choices for Environment and Development"</a>. <i>Our Common Future: Report of the World Commission on Environment and Development</i>. Oslo<span class="reference-accessdate">. Retrieved <span class="nowrap">27 March</span> 2013</span>. <q>Today's primary sources of energy are mainly non-renewable: natural gas, oil, coal, peat, and conventional nuclear power. There are also renewable sources, including wood, plants, dung, falling water, geothermal sources, solar, tidal, wind, and wave energy, as well as human and animal muscle power. Nuclear reactors that produce their own fuel ('breeders') and eventually fusion reactors are also in this category</q></cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Ajournal&amp;rft.genre=unknown&amp;rft.jtitle=Our+Common+Future%3A+Report+of+the+World+Commission+on+Environment+and+Development&amp;rft.atitle=Chapter+7%3A+Energy%3A+Choices+for+Environment+and+Development&amp;rft.date=1987-03-20&amp;rft.aulast=Brundtland&amp;rft.aufirst=Gro+Harlem&amp;rft&#95;id=http%3A%2F%2Fwww.un-documents.net%2Focf-07.htm&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3ARenewable+energy" class="Z3988"></span></span>

<li id="cite&#95;note-337"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-337">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFSiemens1885" class="citation journal cs1">Siemens, Werner (June 1885). <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://zenodo.org/record/1779030">"On the electro motive action of illuminated selenium, discovered by Mr. Fritts, of New York"</a>. <i>Journal of the Franklin Institute</i>. <b>119</b> (6): 453–IN6. <a href="/wiki/Bibcode_(identifier)" class="mw-redirect" title="Bibcode (identifier)">Bibcode</a>:<a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/1885FrInJ.11953IN3S">1885FrInJ.11953IN3S</a>. <a href="/wiki/Doi_(identifier)" class="mw-redirect" title="Doi (identifier)">doi</a>:<a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://doi.org/10.1016%2F0016-0032%2885%2990176-0">10.1016/0016-0032(85)90176-0</a>. <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://web.archive.org/web/20210506135319/https://zenodo.org/record/1779030">Archived</a> from the original on 6 May 2021<span class="reference-accessdate">. <li id="cite&#95;note-338"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-338">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text">Weber suggests that the modern economic world will determine the lifestyle of everyone born into it "until the last <a href="/wiki/Hundredweight" title="Hundredweight">hundredweight</a> of fossil fuel is burned" (<i><a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="http://www.zeno.org/Soziologie/M/Weber,+Max/Schriften+zur+Religionssoziologie/Die+protestantische+Ethik+und+der+Geist+des+Kapitalismus/II.+Die+Berufsethik+des+asketischen+Protestantismus/2.+Askese+und+kapitalistischer+Geist">bis der letzte Zentner fossilen Brennstoffs verglüht ist</a> <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://web.archive.org/web/20180825212624/http://www.zeno.org/Soziologie/M/Weber,+Max/Schriften+zur+Religionssoziologie/Die+protestantische+Ethik+und+der+Geist+des+Kapitalismus/II.+Die+Berufsethik+des+asketischen+Protestantismus/2.+Askese+und+kapitalistischer+Geist">Archived</a> 25 August 2018 at the <a href="/wiki/Wayback_Machine" title="Wayback Machine">Wayback Machine</a></i>).</span>

<ul><li><a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://energypedia.info/wiki/Energypedia">Energypedia</a> – a wiki platform for collaborative knowledge exchange on renewable energy in developing countries</li>
